description:
Teploie, "Las colinas de hierro" ("The iron heights") is a wargame included in Alea Magazine number 7 (March-April 1990).

The game portrays the battle for the Teploie village and heights during the July 1943 Kursk offensive. The German units are part of Army Group Center. One hex represents 1 km.
Game includes a small map (42x29 cm), one countersheet with 160 counters, and eight pages of rules. The CRT and Terrain Effects Chart are printed on the back of the map, so they should be photocopied. Next Alea (number 8, May-June 1990) includes errata, additions and clarifications for the game.

In this game, Conni and her friends go for the first time to the Kindergarten.
Everyone has to hang his or her coat on a special coathanger, that is marked with a special symbol.

All players try to find the 8 images that make up the coathangers in the classroom by selecting a card and flipping it.
If the image revealed is the same as one of the missing images on that player's card, then he or she can take the card and place it on the card.
The first player to find all 8 images for his card wins the game.

The object of the game is to build and complete herds of sheep, thus gaining points for you partnership (or individuals if playing so). The point values are determined by the composition of the herds. While points are earned for all herds, completed herds earn more points than non-completed herds.
There are also rules for two players.

From the Website:

The World of Valon, home to Flintloque and Slaughterloo, goes to Amerka the New Worlde.

The Frontear book contains a full background (more than sixty pages) to the New Worlde, covering the discovery of Amerka to the Pilgrimes and then the Amerkan Rebellion before moving onto the War of Eighteen Bells and then the titantic clash of the Civile Warre and the Wylde West.  The book also contains Full Skirmish Rules covering all these periods, an Amerkan Weapons section, three starter scenarios (for supplied miniatures) tables and all needed statistics, counters and more!

Based on the same D10 engine as Flintloque the Skirmish, this game will be instantly 'user friendly' to all who have play Flintloque and will take only one game to learn for those who have not.

Also included are, dice and twelve painted metal 28mm miniatures with textured metal round bases. Wild Bill Hickorc, Pisol Petro, Deputy Dawg, Vickars and the Dark Elf bandit gang led by disgraced Elf Souffern Confederate Officer Paule La'Regreet.

boardgame: Alice in Wonderland: A Card Game
description:
This game is based on Alice in Wonderland, containing the following cards:

3 Caterpillar, 4 Duchess, 4 White Rabbit, 4 Executioner, 4 Gardener, 5 Knave of Hearts, 4 Cheshire cat, 5 Alice, 4 Queen of Hearts, 6 Mad Hatter, 4 King of Hearts, and 5 March Hares

This is a unique combination of dexterity and wargame.  You drop clay 'bombs' through holes in the provided airplanes and hope they stick to the target locations on the board.  Earn points for completing the bombing missions during World War II.

Poph-Itt TOASTED WHIFFS cereal premium introduced the game "Bomb Tokyo" which was made by the Broughton Specialty Co. in Minneapolis, Minnesota in 1942. It was named "Game of the Year" in 1942. The game was distributed by the Poph-Itt Cereal Company of Minneapolis as a mail-away premium. It was shipped rolled in a solid cardboard tube. The playing field measures about 16" by 22" with a full-color artwork layout of city of Tokyo and its harbor. The board illustrates factories, the emperor's palace, residential areas, and ships.

The object of the game is to drop round bombs made from modeling clay (which was not included) on different areas of Tokyo and the ships in the harbor to score various points. Game includes 4 thin card stock airplanes that come apart (fuselage and wings), each with a "sighting hole" and place to drop your clay bombs. It includes paper playing pieces marked "destroyed" that are placed on the areas that have been destroyed by your bombs.

This golf game comes in a blue box. There is a wheel and a green table in the center of the board and several tracks go up the side of the table, around the wheel, and then back down the other side of the table.  Graphics of golfers surround the functional parts of the board.

Perhaps the earliest bowling boardgame, this game's box features a woman bowling while wearing a dress.  The board is a grid of 48 squares with various numbers of pins, spares, strikes or fouls listed.

This is a Green Acres themed roll-and-move game, with the track going around the outside of the board like Monopoly.  The object of the game is to be the first player to buy enough equipment to start running your farm.

The Serial Killer Trivia Game features Ted Bundy, the Green River Killer Gary Ridgway and Spokane's serial killer Robert Yates. The game places the players in the middle of some of the most notorious cases of our time with a roll of a dice.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description: Gambit is a trick taking game where points are awarded not only for the tricks taken, but also for the card that wins the trick.

An expansion kit for Advanced Squad Leader.  But, this is not a historical module.  It covers a horror story filled with zombies.

The booklet covers the chapter Z "zombies" which describes the rules for zombies and also for human units who fight the zombies.  The zombies are represented by Japanese units.  Human units are represented by other country units, British units for National Guard , German units for Law Enforcement, American units for Armed Civilians.  The chapter also covers the strategy and tactics of these special situations.

6 scenarios are included.

The Orkin Men
Dinner's Waiting
Killing Fields
Hell and High Water
Women & Children Last
Matchstick Men of the Fighting O

Expands

    Advanced Squad Leader

This game is attached with Tactics magazine #66 in Japan. This game is fantasy multi-player game covering the era of reconstruction after the long and chaotic war against dragons.
 This is the first of Okamoto's triology.  The second is Victory in the Galaxy covering sci-fi and the third is Shadow of K's Birlcess covering horror.
These 3 games are real gem which is truely original at the date.  Unfortunately even in Japan these games are almost forgotten, because these games were attached with not the boardgame issue but with the RPG issue of Tactics magazine.  So that, most Japanese board gamers had no chance to see these games.

This game works like Tic-Tac-Toe combined with playing pieces that look like checkers, but move like a king in chess.  It is played on a 4x4 grid, with each player setting up their pieces  in a row on opposite sides.  On each turn, a player moves their piece one space orthogonally or diagonally.  The object is to get 4 in a row orthogonally or diagonally on any row other than your starting row OR to get all your pieces placed in each corner.

The game has some really attractive bakelight pieces.

All those business cards that people hand you can finally be put to good use! PowerLunch(tm) is a two-person game where these cards can be used to battle each other for the one thing they are best suited to attain: a free lunch.

Each player draws 5 business cards from a pool of at least 20.  Cards are then flicked across the play area and if they land near an opponent's card, they attack using the digits of the main phone number on the card.

Cards are classified as one of three types:
1. Free Agents: Someone who is self employed or freelance (or don't specify their employment).  These get two moves per turn.
2. Chiefs: Any card from an executive or someone in an obvious leadership position.  These get +1 to attacks if they touch their opponent.
3. Underlings: Anyone else.  These may be attached to a Chief, and then moved (by hand) to the Chief's new position after he/she is flicked.
This old McLoughlin game features an eagle on the cover holding a large medallion.  The contents are basically cardboard scrabble tiles (minus the numbers), used to work out anagrams.

boardgame: Ironrod: The Search for the Tree of Life
description:
Ironrod: The Search for the Tree of Life is an LDS game based on Lehi's vision of the tree of life in 1 Nephi 8 (from the Book of Mormon). The board features a weaving path that goes through the Iron Rod, the Mists of Darkness, the Great and Spacious Building, and the River of Filthy Water. Players draw colored cards that determine what square s/he will go to next. Some special cards allow you to skip ahead, go to a certain part of the board, or force you to go backwards on the path. The winner is the first player to reach the tree of life.

From the publisher: Around 600 BC there lived a prophet named Lehi. Lehi dreamed about a straight and narrow path that led to the Tree of Life. In the center of the path was the Ironrod. By clinging to the Ironrod and pressing forward, one could reach the Tree of Life. Along the path, one must be very careful not to slip away and get trapped in the Mist of Darkness, the River of Water, or the Great and Spacious Building. The consequence for slipping away would cause a prolonged arrival to the Tree of Life. In order to win the game, one must reach Lehi and the Tree of Life by taking the shortest path. The surest way to reach the Tree of Life first is to stay on the path and follow the Ironrod.
family: Religious: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

description:
Niagara is a wargame designed to be played on its own or as a vehicle for minitatures campaigns.

Players maneuver on the strategic map using the front sides of the counters.

When a battle is to be fought, a tactical map and the back side of the counters are used.

As a miniatures game, units are moved on a map the same as above. Battles may be resolved using miniatures rules or, if the forces are too small, too unbalanced, or in some other way inappropriate, the battle can be resolved using the "quick combat" method included in the game.

The game is set during the War of 1812, fought in Canada between United Kingdom and the United States of America.

description:
A tactical hockey simulation featuring some of the best teams in the history of the NHL - the 1967 Toronto Maple Leafs, the 1976 Montreal Canadiens, the 1985 Edmonton Oilers, and the 1989 Calgary Flames.

The full game is found in the Canadian Wargamers Journal, Issue 22, Vol 4, No 4, Apr/May 1990.

description:
The flip side of the tiles from the game Down Under (if you own Down Under, you own Sturt's Stony Desert). This is a simple connection game for 2 players only (though the rules say 2 to 4).

Try to connect your two sides of the board using the tiles with various curved paths on them.

The game has two phases. In the first phase, players take turns laying tiles with just two curves on them. These are either Y shaped forks, or two bends around opposite corners. If you let your opponent complete their path in this phase, you're doing very badly.

In the second phase, players start over-laying the 2 curve tiles with 3 curve tiles. This makes it much easier to complete a path. When one player does so, the other still has a chance to lay a 3 curve to disrupt the path. But 3 curves only go onto 2 curves. So by clever placement, you can force yourself into a path with two solutions, so your opponent cannot block both at once.

It's a remarkably simple design, but seems wide open with possible techniques. Tiles can always be placed in any orientation. The clever part is that a valid path cannot follow an oblique turn. You go round bends like a train on tracks. So by placing a fork in the correct way, I can improve my path but block yours. But when you get to lay 3 curves, you can undo the damage.

NOTE: There are no Versions listed under this Game entry, as this is played with the components of Down Under. The Versions are therefore listed under that Game entry.

A supplement to the Spanish Fury miniatures gaming rules:

Field Battle rules for the Wars of Religion, 1560-1603. The rules are intended to cover the Wars of Religion in Europe on a regiment scale. The figure scale is 1:40 and ground scale is 1 inch = 25 yards.

From the publisher:

The Spanish Fury system now has modules for Sieges, Field Battles, and Naval Engagements. The Campaign system is just around the corner. One more element was needed  to finish the project, and that’s Actions! These rules cover conflicts involving at most 2500 men, at the least about 300. All types of low-level fighting are covered; minor battles, ambushes, raids, prison-breaks, cattle rustling and the storming of forts!

The basic unit of the game is the squadron, which is composed of somewhere between 25-50 men. These are often the size of weak companies, but we use the term company to cover the next administrative level, composed of several squadrons, somewhere between 150-250 men. Each company has a Captain, who has great influence on his men. The overall commander of a side is called a Colonel.

We’ve tried to portray just about every troop type in Europe in these rules, from Irish Kern to Spanish Musketeers. One of our favorite elements of this game is its expandability and "accessable chrome". Anyone can add their favorite forgotten troop type, from Cimaroon to Lap scouts riding Reindeer!

A miniatures air combat game which requires a fairly good sized hex map, a couple of 10-sided dice and several 1/285 scale miniature aircraft on sticks. The game included statistics for World War I and World War II aircraft.

Cabo da Roca is situated on the very West tip of Portugal. The lighthouse on Cabo da Roca warns ships following one of the busiest sea routes in the world up to a distance of 100 kilometers. Now, you will put yourself afloat on these sea routes heading towards new adventure.

The game has a Carcassonne-style tile-laying mechanic in which players construct a seascape and port locations turn by turn. As ports appear, players can start buying ships to sail around the sea and trade goods from one town to another. Different types of ships are available, with a pirate ship naturally being one of them, and players try to accumulate the most gold possible through trade and settlement.

This Milton Bradley Game (#4168) was produced in 1918 after America entered WWI. The cover artwork of American soldiers in the field in Europe is superb, but the game itself is simple. It comes with 10 counters (5 of each color) and 1 dial. The game board features 5 lines of 26 dots on each line.

The object of the game is to capture your opponent's men. Players use a spinner to determine the number of spaces they can move on any given turn. With each spin, the player can move any of his five pieces. In order to capture an opposing piece, a player must spin and land by exact count on the opposing piece on that line. There is a "shell hole" on each line that offers safety from capture.

Capturing a piece closes that line, and the capturing player can then place his free piece on any other still open line, increasing the opportunities to spin the exact number needed to capture another enemy piece on that line. The first player to capture all of the opposing pieces wins the game.

'Feast of Warring States Period' is a battle class game of "Battle of Sekigahara(Keityo5/A.D.1600)" that 
might be the most famous fight in the history of Japan.

The game includes 1Map(4 A3 size),460 uncut counters.

20 turn all,It corresponds 1 turn : at 25 minutes.  
1 hex : 150 - 200m

The Feast of Warring States Period is dipicted with a different system with the current civil wars battle class game.

The correction to the combat value changes by length of the fire range of the combat unit. The morale rule　plays an important role in the attack and the defense.The Command Power Movement rule reproduces the variable of the movement.

"Betrayal" is an important element of Sekigahara.In this game, victory or defeat is not decided by "Betrayal" check once.In a word, the victory condition is changed depending on the number colored to the ally and the number of turns.

description:
Game based upon the game show "Supergrips".

You have a wall of combs with opposite sides of the same color.

You have to stick lettersticks behind the combwall and then answer questions relating to the different letters that show up. For each question to one letter that you answer right, you can stick one of your tiles into the wall.

The first player (or team) that can build a continous line between the opposing sides, wins.

1838TL (1838: The Thuringia Link) is an 18xx game set in central Germany. This game takes the basic mechanics from 1830, and adds several new elements.

Unique features include the absence of private companies  and an effectively unlimited amount of money, because the game ends only when a company's share price reaches 516. 
The (weird) map is color-coded because companies are only allowed to build in their historical region.

Overview by Chris Lawson:
Location:     Central Germany 
Corporations:     13 
Privates:     none 
Map Size:     377 hexes (387 total) 
Off-Board:     Three steps (2 / 3-4 / 5) 
Bank:         21520 Thaler 
Tile Colours:     Yellow / Green / Brown 
Stock Market:     2D (Square)

[EN]
This is an standard trivia game... Roll, move and, depending on the area you are, answer a History question.

There are 6 different categories for the question cards: Ancient History, People, Medieval History, Arts & Literature, Modern & Contemporary History and Curiosities.

It was published & distributed by Spanish History magazine "Historia y vida" in 1998.

[ES]
Es un juego tipo trivia estándar. Tiras el dado, mueves y, dependiendo de la casilla en que estés, respondes a una pregunta de historia.

Hay 6 categorías diferentes para las tarjetas de preguntas: Historia Antigua, Personajes, Historia Medieval, Arte y Literatura, Historia Moderna y Contemporánea y Curiosidades.

El juego fue publicado y distribuido por la revista española de historia "Historia y Vida" en 1998.

From the One Page Wars game series.

The players compete to be the first to earn 100 credits by purchasing buses, trains, and planes and using them to transport passengers for hire between the seven cities on the hex grid mapboard.

Each type of transport unit is rated for cost, movement and passenger capacity. There are a limited number of such units available: 10 aircraft, 5 buses, and 3 trains.

In addition, the players can build a portion of the transportation system, as there are no train tracks marked on the board - the tracks may be purchased and placed by the players during the course of the game.

From the One Page Wars game series.

Take the part of a local fire commander and allocate your limited resources to attempt to halt and extinguish a fire sweeping through the valley. You have at your disposal three firefighter units, a fire jumper unit, and a fire suppression aircraft. You may also use "backburn" units to halt the spread of the fire. If you lose any of your firefighters before the entire fire is extinguished, the game is a loss.

From the One Page Wars game series.

"The Dutch and Russian Embassies recently opened up in the lovely new nation of Ha Van Teghi. Mr. Von Watley and Mr. Boris as the respective ambassadors."

"Your goal is to take on one of the peronas and attempt to gain the most influence within the new colony as possible before the end of the year."

Players take turns placing their 12 cubes on the board, seeking to establish positions that will garner the most victory points at the end of the game. The players can also seek to invoke the benefits of King Maki-Maki's assistance.

This is a dice game, with a baseball theme, from the 1950s, which uses two special dice.

From the card that comes with the game:
"B-B Ball Players Baseball Game - Everything Real but the Umpire. 9 innings - 9 minutes. Anywhere. Anytime. 2 to 18 players. Nu-process Parlor Dice Game."

The rules are stated in a few lines:
"Same as baseball.
Batters cast dice in turn until a play is made.
A pair (2 of a kind) is a play.
No pair is a strike.
Double play when 3 OUTS are shown."

Penguins move from ice floe to ice floe. Clever usage of the rolled numbers moves little ice floes which make the course for the 12 penguins - a player moves either his floes or his penguins with one die - the penguins try to reach the large ice floe - but their small ice floes can be robbed by the other players, when a six is rolled.

boardgame: A Few Bricks Short of a Load
description:
From the game box:
The Exciting Game of Visual Identification!
In this fun and exciting party game, players have 3 minutes to identify the objects pictured on the card.

Category topics range from dog & cat breeds, to candy, sports, cars, flowers, birds, animals and much more! There are categories for almost every interest - so everyone can enjoy the fun!

Players write down their answers and score points for each corrected guess. Every time a player obtains 50 points - they get a brick. The player who builds the tallest tower of bricks at the end of the game wins!

With "loads" of interesting objects and images to guess, A Few Bricks Short of a Load will provide hours of social fun and family entertainment!

Need Some Help? Are you a few bricks short of a load?
Use the "Magic Wand" to reveal the answers - but in a "scrambled order". Be careful as to when you get these answer clues as they will cost you half your points!

Bugs Bunny's Hideout Game.  Keep Bugs from his hideout and win!

The object of the game is for Bugs Bunny to race to one of his underground carrot hideouts without being caught.  The remaining players play the role of Elmer Fud and try to capture Bugs before get gets to one of 5 hideouts, which is kept secret by Bugs Bunny throughout the game.  Players spin the wheel and move to one of four colors or “pick a card” which could loose players there turn or move them ahead or back one or two spaces.

If Bugs Bunny makes it to his hideout he wins.  If Elmer Fud catches bugs before getting to his hideout then Elmer Fud wins.

A variant of The Perfect Captain's Spanish Fury, Actions! game system of warfare in 16th century Europe, adapted for recreating battles of the English Civil War, 1639-1651.

The variant adds units for the Parliamentarians, Royalists, and Scots. Also added, and common to both armies, are seasoned troops, trained bands, dragoons, firelocks, and clubmen.
"Who gets the best cards gets the best treasures... The treasure seekers try to receive as many matching cards as possible to have valuable treasure-maps!"

"A strategic adventure game. You have to reach the center of the pyramid 3 times - for this adventure you have 3 different pieces with different features. On your way you find bridges helping you to get closer to your aim, because only over a bridge you get to the next level. Each time you reach the center, the gods give you more power. But be careful: the other players can send you back to the entrance, take away bridges to another place or reach the Ankh before you..."

Components: gameboard, 12 pieces in 4 colors, 4 Horus cards, 4 ankh cards, 7 bridges.

From the manufacturer:

Who is to be the new king of the savannah will be decided by this fierce searching game for 2-4 players. In order to reach the lion temple first the players have to be very observant and quickly spot the animal cards which match the dice.

For ages 5 and up.

From the manufacturer:

Treasures ahoy! Once more Capt’n Look has discovered treasure! His pirates come rushing toward the rail and through their long glasses spot gold, jewels and rum. They have to memorize carefully what they see so that the booty will become theirs. A treasure hunt for 2-4 sharp-eyed pirates.

For ages 5 and up.

From the manufacturer:

Quack, quack! Charly is amazed by all the animals in the zoo. However, through the peepholes of the enclosures Charly can only see parts of the animals. Who will help Charly recognize them correctly? A memory game demanding close observation for 2-5 players.

For ages 4 and up.

From the manufacturer:

Oh dear, Hob Hotchpotch has jumbled up the letters! The eager dwarves try to untangle the chaos. Will they find objects, animals or other motifs starting with the letter searched for? An educational game for 2-4 players all about the alphabet.

For ages 6 to 10.

A 2-4 player game of changing lanes, high-speed turns, and flipping people off.

Game Summary:
Players move their car from row 1 to row 15, avoiding traffic and influencing other cars to get in the way of their opponents.

Components:
A bunch of toy cars (ten or more works well), of roughly the same size.
A six sided die for each player.
One sheet of scrap paper for each player.

Everything needed to play is available in the BGG files section, or at:
Games by Jeremiah

This expansion adds magic-user characters, and allows games with up to 10 players.

Expands:

    Blood Master.

description:
Game about exploration of Burghausen, a little city in the south of Bavaria in Germany.

Each player retrieves two cards from each of three parts of the city to which he has to go. Travelling is done by rolling the dice. If a player reaches one of his targets he has to read the text of the card and follow its instructions that usually favour far away locations and add handicaps to near locations.

Beside the normal movement there exist two special movements: one clockwise around the map by travelling by bus and one counter clockwise by travelling by 'Plätte' (tourist ships that were used for shipping salt along the river in former times.) The special movements normally increase your travel speed but add the risc of too far movements.

After a player has reached all his targets he has to move to the castle of Burghausen (the longest in Europe or probably in the world) in the middle of the game map. There he has to roll the dice for each of the six castle wards. The first player to reach the most inner ward wins the game.

description: This game very closely resembles Hungry Hungry Hippos, except the hippos are replaced by alligators and the lever mechanism that causes the heads to eat the marbles operates a bit differently, requiring more pushing than pulling.  The game includes twenty plastic marbles for the gators to eat along with a rule sheet providing multiple variations on play.

The players try to get 100 points by playing Straights.

A Straight can be open (vulnerable to special cards but more valuable) or closed (immune to specials cards but only worth the half of an open Straight).

Cards can be used to building Straight or for special actions (there is reminder text on the cards). There is a lot of interaction between the players because you have a lot of possibilities to mess with the other players and their Straights.

Components
2-4 Players need 2 Sets of Cards (110 cards) and every player needs a die as action buffer.

If you want to play with more than 4 players you need additional sets of cards.

This is a 3-D pyramid game where you move pieces up and down the pyramid.  It actually ships with three games in the rules that utilize the common movement mechanism of moving around the pyramid (and/or jumping like checkers).

Kaptor - capture all of your opponents pieces

The Crown - have 5 of your pieces fill the top five holes at the top of the pyramid

The Voyage - fill the six bottom slots on the opposite side of the board where your opponent started

Isabelle & Isabeau takes us back to the Mediaeval days when knights were bold and princesses charming! Bit like "families", but the dice make the difference.

Basic Rules:

In this game, you try to win as if you were playing a game of "pure" families - indeed you need to build two families of six similar cards to win. But the dice make things interesting.

There are 3 of them: the first is a Character die that denotes either you or one of your opponents, and the other two are Action dice. Typical actions are: draw a card, place a card, discard a card, steal a card from an opponent, swap hands with an opponent, roll again etc etc.

When it is your turn you roll the dice. You will perform one of the actions on the Action dice, knowing that the other one must be carried out by the person shown on the Character die. You choose who is going to do what, and in what order.

Isabelle & Isabeau game doesn't require any grand strategies, but the game mechanism conjures up some interesting combinations.
You may discard most of your hand only to swap it immediately with another player. You may may need to choose between the lesser of two evils yourself. Memory plays an important role: knowing who has which cards in their hand can be vital. And jokers and "veto" cards add some extra twists and turns.

In short, this is a quick, fun game with lots of "mediaeval" illustrations. Oh, and did you get the pun in the title? No? Time to brush up on your French grammar, then...
Isabelle and Isabeau has just been released in mainland Europe, and will be available shortly in the UK. See www.isimat.fr for details or contact muellbauer@lycos.com

Dr. Arnvie's[tm] Grand Rounds[tm] is a medical-themed trivia game produced by The Vincent Company, Inc. [Gledale, WI].

While the game follows the basic "Trivial Pursuit" approach to game play, every element is based on a medical theme. Even the rule sheet is presented as a "Prescription Form," which notes Refill: PRN (As needed for fun).

Per the rules: "Each player acts as a resident physician. (A resident physician is a doctor who is undergoing specialty training following medical school.)

At the start of the game, each player is given 10 patient cards [which] represent the patients that the player must try to discharge during the course of the game. One patient is discharged each time a question is answered correctly."

The first player to discharge all his/her patients wins.

As per the Trivial Pursuit model, there are color-coded spaces on the board that correspond to question categories. The 4 categories are:
Green-History and Personalities
Yellow-General Medicine
Red-Anatomy & Surgery; Public Health; Preventative Medicine
Blue-Nutrition & Exercise.

In addition, other spaces offer exceptions to the color code system and present players with alternative Bonus and Risk cards that can modify their position on the board or choice of question categories.

The rules conclude with a directive [keeping with the prescription pad theme] for "NO GENERIC SUBSTITUTIONS" and "Keep within reach of inquisitive teenagers & adults."

This is followed with a fairly substantial disclaimer that though this game is based on reputable sources, one should turn to Dr. Arnvie for advice on medical treatment or conditions but should, instead, consult your regular medical provider.

Danger City is a speedy card game where you can win in one of two ways: you can side with the Neighbourhood, or with the Robbers. Either have the fewest robbers in your hand and the most points on the table once the final house has been built – or reveal 5 Robbers at once from your hand, and you have won. Danger City takes its inspiration from so-called “Gated Communities” that one may find in plush suburbs of North American (or English?) cities, and the idea that you must either be a very paranoid person to live in one, or have very good reason to want to protect yourself from someone!

We are taken to an idyllic neighbourhood that is in full-blown development – houses are being built, as well as parks, amenities, services and the like.  But somewhere things go wrong: a gang of robbers have arrived. The neighbourhood takes decisive action and starts to install surveillance systems, to thwart their enemies. But who will win out?

Basic Rules

Players take turns in building the neighbourhood, by playing Neighbourhood cards from their hand onto the table. Each card has a specific value – a Garage is worth 5 points while a Golf Course is worth 15. Cards circulate round the player from player to player: you draw or play a card and then pass one on face down. Lurking in their midst are a rabble of robbers. Chances are you either want to collect them or get rid of them, but you cannot play them like Neighbourhood cards. If you collect 5 of them, you can reveal them all at once from your hand and win. You have “sided with the robbers!”

Otherwise the game ends when a specific number of House cards have been played – twice the number of players. At that point the game stops and the player with the FEWEST robbers in their hand wins. If there is a tie, then Neighbourhood points come into play – whoever has played down the most points wins.

And that’s pretty much it!

"España en llamas" is a special issue of Spanish magazine "Wargames, soldados y estrategia", which also has an English language version called, of course, "Wargames, Soldiers and Strategy".
The magazine has 138 pages and it adapts the Spanish Civil War to the "Flames of War" miniatures rules system.
The first pages are a historical introduction to the conflict,while the rest has several army lists, scenarios and missions for the game.
The magazine also includes an extra boardgame about the Spanish Civil War too, "El eco de los pasos" (see entry).

Game for two players of the battle of waterloo for children. Map divided in squares, simple rules, counters type miniatures and the combats are solved of way similar to checkers.
Components: book of rules,book history,map and miniatures paper.

Perykles is a game about Greek ancient wars against Persian. It comprises a full colour map, counters need to be cut. The rules are in Polish.

Game is based on same system that as Hannibal Ante Portas

Game has 2 main elements
- There is strategical map of ancient Greece and parts of Asia minor, where players control countries, and provinces, gather taxes and recruit armies.  Each territory produces up to fixed amounts of recruitment potential measured in men. Regions differ both in potential numbers and kinds of units that are recruitable.
Units are simple graded - Light/ Heavy infantry, light/ heavy cavalry.

Map movement is by army counters. 
When an army crosses neutral province system of friendliness let players decide possible outcome from war, through neutrality to joining trespasser as ally.
There are rules for sieges, forced marches and pillaging.

-When 2 armies meet, Game is using metagame to play a battle.
Battle board has 12 fields

WORLD WAR I
ww.educationalcards.net
ULES (for 3 or 4 players)
AIM: To collect and arrange six Nation-related card events in chronological order.
Each player represents a Nation and must collect cards whose events are relevant
to their Nation. The timeline must contain the following suits:
One "Pre-War Event" card (Purple cards) - Four "War Event" cards (Orange cards) -
One "Post-War Event" card (Green cards).
Each single card displays the points per Nation. These can be zero to three, where
zero indicates that the event is not very relevant to that Nation and three indicates
that the event is very relevant or decisive. Points are found below the original flags.
Dates are found below the card title. These are usually "month-year" (e.g. March 1915)
but some dates are more 'flexible' and embrace a larger period of time (e.g. April-May 1915,
1915-1917, etc.). All cards contain a "Fact" information.
These rules refer to a full round (game). If time allows, more rounds can be played
and a tournament could be set up. Points are collected at the end of each round

A miniatures rules system designed to recreate garrison warfare in the English Midlands during the English Civil War.

"What we wanted to do in Tinker Fox was to drop you into the boots of a Governor of a district in the English Civil War. A Governor does more than fight battles. He must first and foremost follow his masters’ orders. They are usually vague, and it is up to him to come up with a plan to implement them. He must then try to get his subordinates to carry out his detailed instructions. Unfortunately they often have their own agendas and personalities that he must deal with. He must also run an extensive civil government, which is not directly simulated, but is represented by delays and obstructions they naturally cause to military matters."

"Tinker Fox will generate numerous and varied types of battles with whatever troops are present in the campaign. These can be replayed using our new Very Civile Actions! cards now available as a variant for Spanish Fury, Actions! Let the games begin!"

The components consist of a rulebook, quick reference data sheet, unit control sheet, mapsheet and a set of counters.

Battlefinder! is a campaign generation supplement for use with the various miniatures rules released by The Perfect Captain. It is  also suitable for use as a campaign generator for other gaming systems set in Western Europe, for the period 1000 to 1700 A.D.

"It is in essence a modular campaign board that can be used in thousands of configurations. You can use this in conjunction with our miniature rules sets (Ironbow, Spanish Fury, Actions! and Spanish Fury, Battle! or Pistolado!) or with any other set you might own (for those of you who dare to touch a non-Perfect Captain product…you know who you are!). This set can be used in a variety of ways, some of which I’m sure you’ll develop yourselves - and if you do develop something, don’t forget to write it up for our Yahoo group, and share it with us all."

"The cards included with this campaign generator can be used to simulate most areas of Western Europe from the Pyrenees to the edge of the Eurasian Steppes, and from the Alps to Scandanavia."

The Battlefinder! components include a rulebook, a mapsheet, terrain cards and a set of counters.

Like all games from The Perfect Captain, Battlefinder is available for free download to print and play. (See Web Links.)

(From the back of the box) Republocrat is a game which combines a basic knowledge of American History along with luck and skill. Players travel around a circle of spaces named Democracy Boulevard to work towards their ultimate goal: Election to the Presidency.  the spaces on the board allow the players to consecutively move into 5 offices prior to reaching the final stage of the game.  The final stage of the game finds players at the National Convention and the White House. Players may suffer setbacks or gain sudden rewards during the game. Depending if the player answers the question correctly determines if the player moves ahead. In addition, when a player receives a "Republocrat Card", it may require the player to move ahead or go back as indicated on the card.

In this game, players manage stores in New York City.  Stores are established in spaces on the apple-shaped board, and players manage their inventory by holding onto cards representing goods.  These cards are purchased from warehouse spaces and frequently change hands since a player is obliged to purchase a card whenever he or she lands on an opponent's store.  The maximum price which may be charged for an item depends on the number of floors in a store, so players are constantly expanding their outlets.

The game deals with more than just cash -- every player gets a balance sheet used for tracking bank loans and credit with other players.  Credit limits are determined at the start of the game and affect the length of the game -- a $300 limit is recommended for an hour-long game, while $500 can last two hours or more.  As players reach their credit limits and are unable to pay back their creditors, they go bankrupt and drop out of the game.

In addition to the normal flow of buying and selling, the game features board spaces and cards that spice up gameplay with random events.  There are also some special transportation spaces (taxis and subways) which allow players a little more control over their movement to add variety to the normal rolling and moving.

Summary
An abstract game in which the numbers 1-16 have to be arranged into rows, using five auxiliary columns and careful planning.

Aim of the game
Each player tries to lay three new rows, each of them starting with 1 and ending with 16, with all the numbers in between in their correct sequence. The player who first completes his/her three rows wins the game.

Game play
Each player gets 48 number tiles (three sets of numbers 1-16) and arranges them in three horizontal rows from 1 to 16. One player is selected as the caller, shuffles his/her tiles and draws them one by one from a bag. He/she calls the number drawn. Each player takes that number from one of his/her horizontal rows. If it is a 1, he/she can start one of his/her three new main rows. If it is any other number, it has to be placed in an auxiliary column first.

Each player may have up to 5 vertical auxiliary columns of any length. There must not be any gaps (i.e. tiles must be placed directly below one another), and tiles must not be rearranged later. Tiles need not be placed in sequence, but they should be placed in a way that allows for efficient removal to construct the main rows. Tiles may only be taken from the auxiliary columns starting at the bottom (not in the middle or top), and main rows can only be started when a 1 has been called.

Each of the three new rows has to be started with a 1. The tiles have to be placed in correct sequence here. A 3 can only be placed after a 2, and so on. Tiles can be taken from the auxiliary columns (without being called again) as soon as the number at the bottom directly follows one at the end of one of the player’s main rows. E.g. if there is a 3 at the end of one of the player’s main rows and a 4 at one of his/her auxiliary columns, the 4 may be taken and placed next to the 3 in the main row. Tiles can be taken from the bottom of the auxiliary columns as long as they are in matching sequence.

The player who first completes his/her three rows wins the game. If no player is able to complete all three rows by the time the caller calls the last number, the player with the fewest tiles left wins instead.

This is a very simple game which can be played on a sheet of paper or with real matchstick. You draw (or lay) a random number of matchsticks (behind each other) on the sheet. Now one of the two players starts by taking one up to three (which can be choosen) matchsticks away. The other player does this as well and so on. The player who takes away the last matchstick loses the game.

Winning strategy: The person who starts will be able to win the game, when the amount of the beginning isn't x * 4 + 1 (where x may be every natural number). When this is the fact and the person who plays second is smart, he'll win (read what to do). But what to do when you (person who starts) can build a winning strategy?

Take away a specific amount of matchsticks (1 up to 3 of course), so the amount of matchstick still on the sheet is x * 4 + 1 (where x may be every natural number). Now make sure that in two steps (so one turn of you and one of your opponent) four matchsticks are taken away. Which means that when your opponent takes away 1, you take 3, when your opponent takes away 2 you take 2 as well and when your opponent takes away 3 you take 1. Now your opponent will always lose the game.

To try this strategy out you can play the game online: http://www.schoolbits.nl/extern-spel-13b.html

The computer starts, but doesn't directly take the winning strategy. When you make a mistake on your first turn, the computer will take the winning strategy after this turn.

Wargame published in Spain. Edition with few means, is an incunable. It reflects the Catalan conquest from Majorca to the Muslims in 1229.
Components: book of rules, dice, map and four counter sheets.

Feud is all about combat.  It is about warfare - the weaponry, the armor, the tactics, the respite, the strengths and weaknesses of participating warriors, and the suspense and mystery of an unknown outcome.  A typical game can last anywhere from ten minutes to two hours.  It is for two players, and, best of all, Feud is played with two standard decks of playing cards!  This makes Feud cheap!  And sometimes free, (the best kind of game), if you already have the cards lying around.

Feud is initially quite complex.  No one can master the concepts of Feud by playing just a few games, let alone the subtleties.  Once you have played it enough though, you will not only find it enjoyable, but rather simplistic and easy.

To ease new players into the game, a set of Basic Rules is outlined first.  Play several games of Feud with the Basic Rules.  Once you have grasped them soundly, the Advanced Rules will bring the true excitement of Feud to light.  Though we have called them "Advanced" rules, they are just an extension of the Basic Rules, and complete the game's symmetry.  If you find yourself having fun with just the Basic Rules, or Basic Feud, no worries!  Many people enjoy Basic Feud more than Advanced Feud, or True Feud.  But we urge you to try out True Feud if you have enjoyed basic Feud, especially if you like games with higher degrees of skill and lesser degrees of chance.

It is a trick-taking game with 52 honey-bee cards around two rivalling honey bee-colonies.
The player with 1000 points wins the game.

The players travel through the federal state of Hesse, Germany.
They receive one town card which becomes their "home card".
They get 11 more cards which they have to organize for their travel from town to town.
The destination is the capital Wiesbaden.
The die lets the pawns move. A "free time" card is taken when a 6 is rolled. It contains events that either have to be fulfilled immediately or it can be kept to fulfill later. "Free time" cards disrupt the normal trip through Hesse.
The first player to reach Wiesbaden and get rid of all their cards, wins.

4 families sell themselves mutually old treasures, antiques from their market tables for prizes, as high as possible.
The buyer is willing to pay just a little, so the haggling starts. From time to time anything will be stolen, but noone  wants to have seen it. When you have luck you can trade with a partner. You foist a pretty worthless object on the other and get an expensive antique for it. Your partner hasn't known this though.
This and much more makes "Der Dieb von Maradad" a unusually diversified game. Winner is the player who owns the most money after a certein time (=2 hours).

From the web site:

The Fighting Fantasy™ collectible card game (CCG) is a collectable card game based on the Fighting Fantasy™ series of interactive gamebooks published by Wizard Books. In the Fighting Fantasy™ CCG, each player assumes the role of a character card as they attempt to destroy the other player and take control of Allansia. During the course of play, this character will discover secret artifacts, learn new spells and gather monsters to their fold.

The web site was last updated in 2005: http://www.fightingfantasygamebooks.com/tradingcards.htm

Buy Microbadge

Mice are building themselves a racetrack with a start and a finish line. In the middle there is a "cheese-tunnel" out of swiss cheese which the ate 3 tracks inside. Through these tracks they push differently colored cheese pieces. By the pieces which come out on the other side thay know how many fields they can move forward on the racetrack.

Several scuba divers start from the under water platform for treasure huntingbecause the wreck of an pretty old ship is found. Unmeasurably much money was said to be there.
Each player commands three scuba divers who try to get as much money as possible from the depth.
The winner is the player with the biggest treasure at the end.
the group of the plyers tries to get more points than their opponent, the goblin "Tausendsassa". If thes succeed to get more points by skill and luck she has earned the title "Tausendsassa".

In this game you don't count the fields by the die points, but instead of that the counter with the number of the thrown die is brough tonto th next vacant field.
Each player gets 6 counters of a certain color.
Each player tries to get as many counters as possible on the orange number fields.

When the three red final fields are occupied the game is over.
A category is chosen. The clock is running. The turn player writes fitting words into the Wortwurm on the board. Who has the best ideas and writes the most words, gets the most points.

Where is the small plate hidden?
It has to be somewhere!

Turn by turn the players stab with a small stick into the opponent's lump of modeling clay, always on the lookout for a hidden plate.
As long as it isn't found the player on his turn throws a die and moves his pawn forward on the board. The better the plate is hidden the further a player may push his pawn.

Each player has a track for his pawn. On each field of the track there are barrier stones with the picture of Karlchen. Each player has to bring his pawn over the barrier stones as fast as possible to the finish field.

Idea of this game is to build up a landscape on the board. For this purpose sections of landscape cuttings are placed on the board. The players get points for these placements.
Winner is the player who has the most points when the landscape is finished.

An American Indian game attributed to the Zuni tribe of the Southwestern U.S.

A game for two or four players played on a board made up of 168 squares each intersected by diagonal lines. The board is composed of a 12x12 grid with a line of six starting/home squares parallel to and centered along each edge of the board. The players place their six pieces in these squares at the start of the game. In addition, each player has a seventh piece, which is slightly larger, known as the Priest of the Bow.

The object is to cross the board and occupy the opposing home spaces, capturing as many men as possible on the way. Moves are made one square at a time along diagonal lines, with the pieces being placed at intersecting points. The pieces may not be moved backward.

When two pieces surround an opposing piece, it is captured and removed from the board. The first captured piece is replaced by the capturing player's Priest of the Bow, which has the ability to move either on the diagonal lines as normal, or at right angles.

The eggs lay hidden in the nests. Each player has to find out where the 3 eggs of his color are located. Whoever has collected 3 "Nest cards", is allowed to take his eggs out of the nests. But, woe, you got the egg of a cuckoo!
Each player who has collected 3 nest cards, is allowed to turn his 3 eggs over during his turn. Winner is the player with 3 found eggs.

Memory game.  Players move any bear around the wheel attempting to find the matching sized knife, fork or plate. If it doesn't match, you don't get it, and the next player goes. First player to 3 identical sized pieces or any six tiles wins.

Each player tries to get as many animal cards as possible. Important is to achieves getting all four of cards of a specific animal. This gets more points and because the cards are secure when you have all 4 of them, they cannot be taken over by other players.
The winner is the player who has won more quartets of cards after a certain time.

Each player cares for eight animals of one species. On the board they lie all scattered around. The player who manages first to see all of his animals at the same time on the board has almost won. He has to answer a question and wins.

Skat may be played by 2, 3 or 4 players. It is similar in mechanics to Sorry! with dice in place of cards and marbles in place of movers. The players roll a die in hopes of throwing a 1 to leave their start space in the moulded plastic game base. Traveling the track of the board, it is a race to see who can get all 4 of their marbles into their home first. When you land on a space occupied by another player you call out "skat" and send that player back to their starting spot.

In a 4 player game, you can play as teams with the goal of getting the 8 team marbles back prior to the other team. Once a player has all 4 of his in the home spaces, he can begin to move his partners marbles.

Another version of the game is Wild Skat, using an extra hole in the center of the board. This extra hole allows you the opportunity to cross the board in fewer moves, but increases your chance of being "skatted" by your opponent.

Content: 90 playing cards, 9 joker, 1 big mouse

Each player tries to get rid of his own cards (=mice). All players play simultaneously and put down their cards on the 3 to 4 decks on the table. The first player who get s rid of his cards catches the mouse, and wins the game.

Kernel stones and the one stable stone are put together in a circle on the table.
The "Küken" (baby chicken) is placed on the stable stone. Each turn the player throws the die, and the Küken is moved. When it passes the stable stone, the turn player may take one kernel stone out of the circle.
The player with the first 3 colored kernel stones wins.

The idea is to catch as many fish and as a big a fish as possible, but also there are many items that lie in the water, but do not belong there. The winner is the player with the highest card value at the end.

Sucking thumb is a dreadful habit. Every child knows this. It is hard to get rid of this habit. This game provides 15 situations that you can come into such an embarrassing situation. Each time when your pawn puts a thumb into his mouth you are  punished by slowing down your forward progess. Who will be first to the finishing field without sucking their thumb?

Caution....it burns at several places at the same time! The players act as courageous firemen and try to put out the burning fire as fast as possible. Fast comprehension and skillful planing decide who gets the most firemen merits for successful actions.

From the publisher's website:

The Friday Night at the ER team-learning game is a simulation tool for gaining powerful insight in an engaging group experience. It's a board game played at one or more tables of four players and led by a facilitator. The game teaches people to:
    * consider the effects of their decisions on the larger system,
    * to collaborate with an openness to redesign, and
    * to develop sound data on which to base decisions.

Who is it For?
    * Work Teams, Retreats, Classes....for Improvement Work, Restructuring, Planning, Team Building
    * Any group whose members have to work together
    * Minimum group size is 4; maximum is 200

How Does it Work?
The game simulates the challenge of managing a hospital during a typical 24-hour period. Players perform distinct functions, but they come to realize that they also depend on each other. The game play (2 hours) is followed by an interactive debriefing (1 hour or more).

What Can it Teach?
    * Systems Thinking: managing interdependence, complexity, delay
    * Collaboration: shared accountability, coordinated action across functions
    * Innovation: working with mental models, openness to redesign
    * Use of Data: system performance data, measurement
    * Structural Thinking: designing for performance

Based on The Beer Game

660 incredible stories that are true or a lie.
"Elephants drink water through their trunks .. Is this true or is it a lie?" The Bremen Town Musicians have known fairy tale in the city of Bremen never reached .. etc.

Each player tries to get chips of each color. You get chips when you know after a story is read out whether it is true or false. Eight chips let you win the game.

Contents: 

     110 cards with a total of 660 true-false stories, 
     dice, 
     winning chips

The 24 lovely picture cards of this game are telling 8 depicted mini-stories (each within a sequence of 3 cards). You play with as many sequences as players are taking part (4-8). At the beginning of a game the redundant cards are sorted out and the rest is shuffled well. Each player gets 3 cards on hand. By shifting simultaneously one card to the left neighbor the players try to get first a complete sequence. When this happens to you, grab quickly and secretly a chip from the table. The other players immediately also catch a chip. Only the last and slowest player gets nothing, and a new game begins. After some turns (at least as many as playing members) the player with most chips wins.

From publisher's press release:

Based on the classic chess, Siege is played with a standard set of chess pieces, a circular 120-space Siege board, and new objectives.

With its debut at the 2008 Canadian Toy & Hobby Fair on January 26-28 in Toronto, Siege Games is poised to unleash this innovative new game on Canadian chess players.

"If you can play chess, you can play Siege", says Frank Elstub, President of Siege Games. "Where chess represents two opposing armies facing off across a field of battle, Siege moves the conflict to the siege of a castle. The pieces move basically the same as in chess, so it's easy to get started, but there are more spaces and new perspectives, so standard chess strategies are out the window."

boardgame: Race for the Galaxy: The Gathering Storm
description:
The Gathering Storm is the first expansion for Race for the Galaxy and adds new cards, an additional player, goals to compete over, as well as solitaire and drafting rules.  It also includes some blank cards for players to make their own cards.

The new cards include four start worlds, three 6-cost developments, two other new developments (and one duplicate development), five military worlds and six non-military worlds (including a replacement gambling world).  Goals consist of  ‘First Goals’, which reward the first player to achieve an objective with 3 extra VP, and ‘Most Goals’ reward the player with the most developments, military or certain types of worlds.  An extra set of player cards and VP chips allows for a 5th player, and a robot template, dice, and chits allows for a solitaire game.  Finally drafting rules provide a different way of playing the game for 2 or 3 players.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: A reflector-box is put in the middle of the table. All plates are arranged in any order on the 20 spots of the board edge. The turn player pushes two of the plates in the middle of the box to see the down side. If he sees 6 symbols at the upper and the mirror side he can take this plate.

The game is a memory game for Young and Old. You will get to know many species of local birds and trees. The players let male and female birds of the same species fly in the same kind of trees.  Besides good memory, tactical skill and luck are necessary for the winner.
Each player tries to get as many bird pairs as possible .

Each player tries to bring 5 freight containers to the space stations with his space ship. The players block each other though, and the destination computer is playing up. Winner is the player who has delivered his freight containers and returned back to the basic field first.

Every day courageous sailors go on a dangerous trip to the fishing areas of the world. They throw out their nets and hope of the biggest catch of their lives.
In this game the winner is the player who gets the most points out of his catch before an arranged number of fishing trips are completed.

The window board is placed between the players. The turn player throws the symbol die, takes a marker and throws in into a slit with the same symbol. From now on the turn player has to memorize first how many markers are in every slit. Is his answer correct, He may throw the die once more. Is it wrong. His turn is over. The winner is the player who wins 4 rounds.

Iron Road is the original printing of the game that would become TransAmerica.

Iron Road differs from TransAmerica in several ways though, for instance, the board is made up of squares rather than triangles, and the scoring system and game end conditions are altered as well.

Re-implemented by:

    TransAmerica

Starfire, The Gorm-Khanate War was an expansion to Starfire 2nd Edition (Boxed Set). This expansion introduced new rules, and a new race and empire to the game, the Empire of Gormus. In addition, it provided a campaign based on the war between Gorm and the Khanate of Orion using the campaign expansion Starfire: New Empires for Starfire 2nd Edition.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description: Each player moves the eggs of his color over the colored field of the board - towards a strange nest. Destination is to lay your own egg as a "kuckoo egg" into the nest of another player as fast as possible. Who manages this first wins the game.

During the drying season all kangaroos are very thirsty. It is necessary that they reach the next water hole. The kangaroo pairs, mother and baby, must overcome the obstacles and reach the rescuing water hole. Those who reach the water first, may drink first. The player of the first kangaroo wins.

Each player slips into the role of an ice bear who jumps from floe to floe. In the meantime the ice bears made a game out of it. They count the floes which they reach without falling into the water. The players take the floes which they leave and add the points of them. When no one can make any move the game is over. The player with the highest added points wins.

The small gnomes are smart friends. They jump from tree bloom to tree bloom, schmooze here and there and rest on a mushroom.
Each player tries to let their gnomes run through the woods so skillfully that their own forest plates are used and put on the table in a 5 x 5 pattern as maximum.  
The winner is who returns back to the gnome house first.

Downwards goes the one lift, upwards the second one. In the new warehouse you can see marvelous things to buy. The skillful user of the lift will soon have full shopping bags first. The player who returns to the entrance hall with full bags first wins the game.

Nice to look at it....the good old steam locomotive! With much noise she moves through the landscape and brings passengers  to their vacation areas. We arrange a race and to give every player the chance to travel with the express.
Each player has 3 pawns and 3 secret tickets. The tickets show where the pawns have to travel with the Holiday Express. The winner is the player who reaches his destination with his pawn first.

48 cards are shuffled, and each player gets 6 cards. One card is put face-up on the table. The cards contain up to 6 different fruit. The turn player has to put down one card onto the open file. 
When the open card shows: 3 bananas, 3 strawberries and 3 pineapples, the player must have a card with at least 4 bananas or 3 strawberries or 3 pineapples.
The winner is the player who gets rid of his/her cards first.

Many rabbits hide themselves in the colorful flower meadow. Also foxes are hidden there though ... and secret subsurface ways. You must try to find the 4 rabbits of your own color and bring them home into their stable. The player who has rummaged up his 4 rabbits first wins the game.

The animals get along well together. Sometimes they talk about who is the bigger animal. 
In this game before the turn player opens up the next card he has to foresee or to guess: "Is the next open animal bigger or smaller than the animal that is already open on the table?" For a right guess you get chips. 
The player who has filled his chip board totally wins the game.

Each player tries to reach the beehive with his bee first. The honey bees fly from bloom to bloom. They may move in every direction. Does a bee meet another bee, this one must go back to the start and begin anew.
The bee which reaches the flying board of the beehive wins the game.

Double is a cute color domino. 
The game has 72 cards. Each card is subdivided in 3 colored sectors with a card value from 1 to 7.
The player has to get rid of his cards as fast as possible by putting them to the both ends of the open card line: yellow part aside yellow, red aside red, a.s.o.
The Player who gets rid of his cards first is the winner.

It appears that this game is very similar to, but not the same as On the Double, also by Maureen Hiron.

"Amusing, easy party & family game. Collect Element stones for the Elements Fire, Water, Earth and Air. But be careful: you will find Dangers like Volcanoes, Floods, Earthquakes and Hurricanes. If you don't escape you are lost! Who will be Lord of the Things?"

Components: four game boards (earth, water, fire, air), 16 danger cards, 84 element stones (21 of each color), 4 bonus cards, and 16 player pieces (4 of each color).

http://www.printgames.net/english/detail_e/lord.htm

An abstract strategy game played on a board with sixty squares bearing numbers from 1 to 6.

"A 'spacy' party & family game. Create a whole 'NETWORK' with Satellites, Net-markers, Rockets, clever actions and a little luck... You need a DIE too!"

The object of the game is to be the first player to create a 'network' by placing satellites and your net markers.

Components: gameboard, 6 satellites, 6 rockets, 24 net markers in four different colors.

http://www.printgames.net/english/detail_e/network.htm

2 to 4 small dragons always run to a volcano in order to get fresh fire stones for their store cupboard.
On the way there are surprising trade transactions.  Which dragon has the most fire stones at the end?

Each player takes one of the meadows. Then he takes one butterfly with 2 holes in their wings. Simultaneously all players try to find a position on the board where the color which shines through the hole is the same as on the other wing side.
The player who finds it first wins, and the next round can begin.

The dinos are on their way to modern times. They have to overcome obstacles along the way. Hexagonal plates are put into a cue on the table. They are the playing board. Who has struggled through all events, and has reached the modern times?

Welcome to Wimmelburger -Restaurant.

Here you can make your hamburger yourself, but there are not only tasty ingredients, but also uneatable ones that can be on that burger.
Before you finish a hamburger by putting the "upper half card" on the open deck you should convince yourself that you don't have any minus cards inside. The player with the most ingredients cards wins.

From the One Page Wars game series.

The players compete, as Ramses I and Ramses II, to become the most well known and pious pharaoh of the ages. Using a limited supply of "build points", the players build pyramids, obelisks, statues and temples in and around Egypt. The Ramses II player also has the opportunity to denounce or deface the monuments of his predecessor. After the players have exhausted their build points, they determine when and if each monument is discovered (by die roll for the years 1880, 1912, and 2006). Points are scored based on the piety value of the buildings and the grandeur of buildings discovered in the future, bestowing each player's pharaoh with titles ranging from "The Great" to "The Weak."

People in the zoo are excited: The food for the animals ran out. All children work together to collect food in baskets and carry it to the zoo by truck (=Brummi). Whoevers basket reaches the zoo first, is the winner.

In old Peru in the Inca empire King Atahualpa gave some of his people a brain teaser. Inside his palace he hid 34 animal symbols on a certain floor area.
When all symbols are solved each player counts his gold treasure.
Each player tries to hide his dino eggs while at the same time trying to find his opponents dino eggs.  The winner is the player who best hides the most eggs.

The idea is to win a MiniT (=Mini-Tennis) Tie-Break.
The game has 56 position cards, 20 running cards, 32 forehand cards, 32 backhand cards.

The game starts with the usual serve of a normal Tie-Break.
The played cards are put down on the table so that a pattern of cards is being developed into the size of a tennis court  (8 by 5 cards). Player A starts with his serve. Then the players alternate getting 2 serves, starting with player B.
Play continues to 7 points or until a player leads by 2 points once 7 is reached.

4 basic arithmetic operations are exercised in this game.
Each player gets cards with a math problem. Whoever has solved all his problems first wins the game.

With this quiz game the players will learn everything that they always wanted to know about the world of good cooking. With this game another lifestyle trend arrives now in the world of boardgames. „Which is a class champagne? “, „who is a famous television cook in England? “, „are there also female star cooks?“. These are only some the 600 questions, which were developed together with German Star Cook Johann Lafer.
The map is a genuine porcelain plate of Villeroy & Boch.

boardgame: Die kleinen Biber bauen einen Damm
description: Together all of the beavers try to save the dam from the rising water. They get logs out to the woods in order to repair the beaver dam. Only when the beavers are fast enough will they win against the water.

Each player tries to get his six markers from the bottom row no. 6 through the sand glass to the opposite row no. 6.
The player who reaches this opposite row first is the winner.

Children's game where they try and create a colored flower.
Three marbles, a bowl and lots of colored petals and the game can begin!
White, red, yellow or blue? Nobody knows what petal the marbles will land on.

Well hidden under the cup three marbles are rolling. For each marble that lands on a colored leaf, you get a petal of the same color.
The first one to collect six colored petals and thus creates a colored flower wins the game.
Each player tries to collect as many points as possible by good placements in the 5 downhill ski-races. The winner is the player who has achieved the highest score after the last race.

boardgame: Mein erstes Naturspiel: Kennst Du den Baum?
description: The players try to lay open the cards of a tree species and to get wood stones for this action.  When all seven tree species in the row 1-5 lay open the game is over, and the player with the most wood stones wins.

A hungry shark is on his way to eat fish caught in the nets of the fishermen. The fishermen decide to work together and hunt this shark. The fisherman are strongest when 5 are in the same boat, so they have to plan how they can get 5  together. The Shark is caught when the 5 fishermen and the shark are together on a light blue field, then the fishermen have won. If the shark makes it to his cave then he has won.

The game is played in 5 rounds. Players take turns flipping over cards. When a banana card is flipped up, the active player gets a big banana. When a monkey is shown, the banana has to be put back. Banana peels mean there is no change. When all cards are face up the round is finished. The player who has the big banana at the end of the round gets a small banana. The player who has the most bananas at the end wins.  
The game is entirely luck-driven and targets the very young.

A trick-taking card game around Agent 006.
The players try to get as many tricks as possible. In the tricks there may be identity cards which can be traded in for agent equipment cards.
The players with 6 or more equipment cards wins the game.

The players discover an unknown island where goblins live. At the and of the island visit they get treasures from the goblins which have to be found around the island. The goblins however have fun trying to  disturb the players during their search for the treasure.
The player who has found the most treasures is the winner.

During unloading of an airplane all suitcases on the plane have been dropped and opened. The contents of the suitcases got messed up. Everyone has since quickly filled the suitcases again, but what is inside my suitcase now? 
The game ends when all passengers have found their suitcases. The player with the highest score wins.
The players try to play a coup of Max & Moritz.  Whoever succeeds in playing a coup may give away a chip.  Whoever gets rid of his chips first wins the game.

Are you "wordy"? Then you will have fun with this fast and funny game. The game idea is to build words out of a "handful of characters". Short words or long words. The longest words let you move quickly forward to the finish line.

The trip starts on the moon and leads to a wild odyssey to the sun. With luck and some playing with numbers you are able to send back some space stations and with them skillfully move yourself forward. The winner is the player who reaches the pink star with the 6 on the sunbeam first.

Three different word games can be played with the material.
As an example the first game:
Playing Passt in jeden Koffer' (="fits in every suitcase") the game leader thinks about a word and puts the matching character cards in wrong order on the table. Each player tries to find the word first to get the attribute card.

With the Lettro - character cards you can play many popular games as Buchstaben-Memo for 2 to 5 players, or Alphabet-Training for 1 and more players,or Buchatebn-Quartett for 2 to4 players, Kreuzwort, ABC-Schnipp-Schnapp and finally Wörter-Poker.

From the One Page Wars game series.

"Welcome to your new farm! The land has been previously farmed pretty hard and it is your turn to make it profitable again."

Players take turns placing their markers, either on the mapsheet or on a strategy sheet (the latter invokes certain special results, such as increasing victory points for occupied hexes, or requiring markers to be removed from the board, etc.) Once both players have placed all twelve of their markers, the positions are scored to determine the winning farmer.

Each player plays the role of a magician who has to brew a magic potion. They need ingredients for it. The magicians walk through the forest to search for those ingredients. In some fields they find only one half of a ingredient and have to look for the other half. The winner is the player who accumulates all of the needed ingredients first.

From the One Page Wars game series.

An abstract, small-format "shoot-em-up" wargame with asymmetrical forces.

The mapsheet is a narrow corridor of irregularly shaped spaces made from overlapping hexes, some of which are marked as offering special defensive bonuses.

The black forces, which start at the south edge of the map, consist of three armor, three infantry, and three artillery units. The red player controls the northern half of the mapsheet, which holds the victory point hexes. The red forces consist of five infantry units and three artillery units.

All of the units are marked with two numbers. To prevail in combat, a unit must roll a result between these two numbers on 2d6. All units have the same movement rate, one space per turn.

Each player must try to get rid of his cards as fast as possible. That happens by laying out his cards in a maximum of 4 rows on the table or on the decks of opposing players   Whoever lays down all his cards first is the winner and takes the camels.

We travel with Tiger and Bear through Panama, the land of their dreams. You smell bananas there, wherever you are. On their trip they meet a lot of friendly animals, go by raft and have to go across bridges in order to finally come back home happy  . In order that no one dies of hunger everyone  gets a cooking pot, anyone else goes fishing at the neighbor... Whoever returns back home first wins.

Each player gets a pawn which is moved by a die and cards with pictures of male birds. The cards of the female birds are put face down on the board 
The players try to find as many fitting cards with female birds as possible. The winner is the player with the most pairs of birds.

Hedgehogs are useful animals, but the fox is one of their natural enemies. They find shelter for themselves by rolling themselves together into a bowl.

A highway with cars is a dangerous place for them. On land without bushes and hedges they cannot hide themselves. The players must try to keep the hedgehogs out of danger. The player who finishes his tour around the board and back to the nest with hedgehogs first is the winner.

In the Indian village Pocahontas looks for friends Meeko and Flit. After she has found them she walks to Grandma Willow's where she gets help for her adventures. Together with Captain John Smith she walks across the rock and the river to the ship. They have to pass the malicious Governor Ratcliffe. Whoever reaches the ship first with Meeko, Flit and John Smith wins the game.

The game idea is to save the earth from suffocation in garbage. The players move over the board hitting fields with question cards and action cards. The right answers provide green chips. 3 chips can be traded in for a puzzle segment of nature that covers the garbage. Wrong answers and bad action cards provide black points which let the clock move towards the 12!
Is the landscape totally filled with nature puzzles the players have won.
The idea of BRAINY is to decode the secret three-line-pattern-code   and to get as many chips as possible to win.
Each player tries to provide Santa Claus   with a cap, coat, sack, pants and boots and to reach one of the red marked finish fields with one pawn.

Publishers description:

"Children of all ages will have fun challenging their spelling skills while playing The Great Word Race! You'll never believe spelling can be such a blast. Race your pawn from start to finish by moving it the number of letters in the word you build. Throw six dice with the letters of the alphabet. Add any Letter for Life cards you have collected. If you land on a Pick A Card square, use it! Should you collect Letter for Life cards, double your spaces, or Pick a Card and follow its instructions. Choose your strategy and race for the finish!"

For 2 to 4 players. Ages 6 and up.

The Darling Dash to Tuck Baby into Bed! It's beddy-bye time and it's up to you to collect a teddy bear,pajamas and bottle and tuck your baby into bed. Before you know it, your precious little bundle of joy will be all snuggled up and fast asleep!
