Several storm cells bear down on the town of Doomsville, Oklahoma (population 213). Each contains an F1 tornado that will grow in strength and carve its own path of destruction. Do what you have always wanted to...take control of one of nature's most dangerous forces: the tornado! Using your twister, destroy as much of the town of Doomsville and the surrounding countryside as you can - do a better job than the other players and win the game!

To win, you will need to properly control the path of the tornado and manage its strength so that destroy high value buildings. Some buildings can only be destroyed by extremely strong F5 tornadoes, while others can be wiped out with only an F2. You will manage the strength and path using a pool of "storm points". Each player starts with the same amount, but as you use them to plan your actions, the pools for each tornado will change accordingly.

Doomsville rebuilds itself for each game; meaning that for each game there is a method for randomly building the town and the surrounding countryside. This makes each game play distinctly different and puts the tornadoes into a fight to get the higher value buildings before their opponents.

So what are you waiting for...? Get to wiping Doomsville off the map!

NOTE: This game was inspired a couple of months after a small twister (F0 probably) tore up some trees in my yard in July 2006 and discussing it with my son. Tornadoes are very dangerous and interesting facts and safety information about tornadoes will be included in the game.

Thomas has challenged James, Molly and Percy to a great race! Learn numbers as you earn coal to shovel into your engine and power it up and over the hill. Develop sorting, counting and color matching skills as you race to the finish.

OBJECTIVE:  
Catch and bring in the highest total weight of fish.

GAME PLAY:
Draw a boat, choose your fishing "jigs" (rods) and lures, then set out to catch fish.  You decide how far to travel (up to your boat's max speed) on each turn, but you have to slow down if you're in the weeds or in a dock zone.

Travel around the water "casting" and "flippin'" fish, and make sure you have the right jig for the type of water you're fishing.  If you're "flippin'", you can get right up next to a fish, but if you're casting, you have to stay a few spaces away and roll the die to see if you've caught it.  (Takes some initial strategy to make sure you have enough of the right jigs for the area you're planning to fish.)

And those fish are crafty - you might lose a rod or a lure or pull in a mermaid or old tire instead of a fish. And sometimes you have to throw one back.

Get back to your starting dock within 32 turns or start losing fish.

Includes rules for "Recreational" play and "Tournament" play.

GAME COMPONENTS:
1 Lake playing surface (board with small hexes)
6 boat cards
48 lure cards
36 "jig" cards (rod and reel)
12 sets of fish markers, of 5 types (two types have 3 sets, the rest have two)
6 Strategy statistic reference cards
1 six-sided die
6 colored plastic round boat markers
1 score pad
15 plastic re-usable bags

From the publisher's website bigboardraiders.net :

I do not have any affiliation with any investment entity and I am not encouraging anyone to invest in anything. This is intended for your entertainment and learning.

I think we all know young boys or girls who seem like they were hard-wired for finance. When the subject of money is being discussed, they are right there like spounges soaking it up. I know because I was one of them, but my parents didn't take the time to teach me about money. Today lots of parents still don't teach their kids enough about money, and yet our kids will all deal with money most days in their lives and how early they begin to understand money, will directly influence their lifestyle. This game is for parents to encourage those little finance prodigies. The world is changing to where the cash in your hand is the crumbs from the table, while the banquet is inside the corporations. For Grade 5 and up, but best for teens and up.

The “Big Board” is like a nickname for Wall Street, while “Raiders” are the teams of entrepreneurs, lawyers, accountants, brokers and investment bankers who engaged in hostile takeovers of the companies they pursue. In Big Board Raiders, players can gang-up by pooling their capital to promote a cause or stop another player, but alliances don’t last forever and when they end, backstabbing is an inevitable strategy. Communication and deal making are very important enhancements to a player’s game strategy. A few choice words can help your portfolio or have the opposite effect on your opponent’s. It’s all about opportunities, timing and the hustle.

There are two main objectives players engage in while playing Big Board Raiders; the first is to get elected by proxy as a director of a company and the second is to grow your portfolio using the many investment vehicles, so that you will have the capital to make your move to control your company; the company in which you were elected to the board by proxy. There are four trading companies: Yellow, Blue, Red, and Green. Six Subsidiaries, and each of these subsidiaries has a proxy vote that it gives to the players as they land on their subsidiary space on the game board. To win, grow your capital while collection the proxy votes to be elected as a director, then buy enough shares to take over control of that company.

This game is a parody of the Fleet Captain’s Gold Hat Championship at Origins. The game reflects the final four rounds, although players could agree ahead of time to play all seven rounds (or any number of rounds). Each player draws opponents and plays various cards on themselves or the other players in an attempt to reach the gold hat before anyone else. The game is intended for 4-6 players and is extremely tongue-in-cheek, concentrating more on the humorous aspects of the game instead of the serious. It is not the intent of the designers or publishers to insult anyone but only to provide an entertaining distraction between four hour long tournament games.

description:
Ming is an original chess-like strategy game. It is played on a board with 7 x 7 squares. 
Every player has 7 figures (1 emperor, 1 mandarin, 2 generals, 3 monks) that vary in height. In the course of a game, own and opponent's marbles are added to the figures. These define the distance a figure can move and in which way (diagonal or horizontal and vertical).

The game is won in one of three ways:
1) Conquer your opponents emperor;
2) Reach the opponents starting line or
3) Conquer seven marbles of your opponents colour.

from Radio Game Show Games: This early effort predates what are generally considered to be the first game shows.  Players drew chips from a bag to determine which word on the host's card they had to spell correctly.  Paul Wing was an author and NBC staff director.  His show was originally heard only in the cities competing in each weekly broadcast, but the program did eventually (and briefly) gain full network status.  Cutting costs even in the 1930s, Milton Bradley's materials for this game were identical to the ones used in Vox Pop.

The game was played by choosing a word caller, then going around the room and having people pull numbered disks out of a bag; the number on the disk determined the word you would spell (and some words were notably easier than others, so it was a bit of a game of chance). Correct moves got you a little green counter called a "honey," and wrong moves got you a little red counter called a "stinger" (because it's a bee, get it?)

PLEASE NOTE: This game description is out-of-date and needs to be updated.

Sturm Europa! is a strategy game for one to five players that depicts World War 2 in Europe. Euro-style wooden pieces are used to depict resources, that depending on placement, represent air, land, and sea units. Each player has a privacy screen that creates "fog of war" by keeping resources and units not on the front line hidden.  Sturm Europa! will distinguish itself from other games of the genre in these key areas:

Playability:  The entire war, Fall 1939 to the bitter end (Spring 1945 or later), can be played in 6-8 hours. No hexes, no counters, and low luck.

Scale:  The map board employs area movement over land regions but the low unit density allows players to realize the full impact of mobile warfare and appreciate the daunting size of Mother Russia without slowing game play unnecessarily.  A unique front-reserve combat system allows for operational level details like blitzes, assaults, encirclements, feints, static defense, and defense in depth which are normally lost in grand-strategic simulations.

Units:  Units are not only rated by movement, but attrition, assault, and blitz strength.  Elite units are created by combat experience, events, and tech research - introducing an extra dimension as units are upgraded and downgraded throughout the war, reflecting varying levels of experience, training, leadership, and technology.

Production:  Sturm Europa! goes beyond the standard model of linking military production to a GDP estimate.  Nations must mobilize to achieve full wartime production but it takes much more to wage war than just massive production lines.  Manpower, oil, steel, and even trained pilots are all tracked without ANY bookkeeping through an innovative system of collecting resources as strategy cards are played.  Resources are consumed by prolonged offensives so logistics plays just as much a factor as unit strength.

Strategy Cards:  Sturm Europa! is a card driven game (i.e. Paths of Glory). Events introduce historical and political events in a realistic fashion while eliminating scripting and "look up" rules.  There is a tangible cause-effect relationship to every event and nothing occurs in a seemingly random or ahistorical manner.  Players play the cards - the cards don't play the players.  Most importantly, the core mechanic of tough gut-wrenching decisions which make CDGs so fun is still preserved.

Technology: Players can customize their units to fight the style of war that best suits their strategy. Germany can develop the He 100 long range fighter that greatly improves their chances in the Battle of Britain. The Americans can develop a heavy-medium tank instead of sending Shermans to get chewed up by King Tigers. Tough decisions will have to be made to implement new technologies though. New designs don't just start rolling off the assembly lines without causing a shortage of replacements on the front line while the factories retool and crews retrain...

TENTATIVE COMPONENT LIST:
(1) 867x668mm six-panel mounted map
(4) storage boxes
(4) player mats
(500) stickered wooden blocks
(150) 2" double-sided strategy tiles
(1) embroidered draw bag
(84) intel and technology cards
(150) 5/8" punchboard counters
(10) custom 6-sided dice
(1) 16-page full-color rulebook

Can be combined with Days of Infamy

From Old Silk Road:  A voyager in the game finds himself on the trade routes which existed at the height of the Tang Dynasty in China (c. 750 AD). He sets out from Chang An, the cosmopolitan Chinese capital. Traveling across northern China and then to Central Asia, with a lucky roll of the die he will arrive at Samarkand, an important trading center. There, glass, fruit and horses may be purchased. At Merv, the traveler must decide whether to follow the overland or sea route. Going by sea, one has the opportunity to buy incense at the port of Hormoz, collect rubies, sapphires and pearls in the kingdom of Anuradhapura (now in Sri Lanka), visit the wondrous temples at Elephanta and Kancipuram and buy spices in Srivijaya (Sumatra). But beware! There are mighty perils on this route. On the overland route, the traveler visits magnificent Buddhist temples in India and beyond, encounter tigers in the jungles of Bengal and purchase luxuries such as kingfisher feathers and rhino horn at Angkor. The final leg of the journey passes through the the bustling port of Guangzhou (Canton) and then off to Japan and Korea where the traveler will be able to visit foreign temples and complete final trading before returning home by way of the Grand Canal.

Each location includes a special paragraph of text describing what happens to the traveler there. Often they include special trading opportunities. Major trading centers have specific products which they sell and also another list which they want to purchase. Commodity holdings are shown by cards which list the differing values of these items at various locations. Includes short history and glossary of terms.

The rabbits eat delicious vegetables in the garden. In front of the garden fence they discover big carrots. In the middle of the garden there is a hole. Caution! Don't fall down there! First there is a thin chain that is laid around all players' pawns which are placed around that hole on the board. Each turn the player throws the die and moves as many fields to the outside as the die shows. When a rabbit reaches the chain it is pushed forward in an outside direction while opposite rabbits located in the opposite direction are pushed towards the hole and may fall into it when the thrown die shows enough eyes. The winner is the player who has at least one rabbit left at the end.

Each player on his turn flies with his magnetic honey bee to any bloom and puts the bee on the pollen in the middle of the bloom. He then lifts the bee up carefully. If he is lucky some pollen will keep sticking to the magnet. Then he may immediately fly back  and deposit the pollen into the honeycomb. The winner is the player who fills up 5 honeycombs first.

Auto-Scooter at a fair: Each player moves his auto-scooter around the track. He tries to crash into the scooters of the other players and gets "crash points". But has to be careful not caution not to get crashed into from the back.
The winner is the one with the most crash points at the end!

Each player drives a taxi cab that they use to pickup passengers from locations determined by dice rolling to a destination determined by a dice rolling.

Payment is determined by the route taken and the distance traveled.

Intersections can provide an advantage or a disadvantage to each player. Intersections can be clear, have lights or be completely blocked, which changes the route chosen by the players.

Players can also get in each others way.

description:
SHERLOCK HOLMES AND THE FENIAN MURDERS

Terrorism was nothing new in 19th century England. Irish Fenian rebels plotted violence in England and abroad. Now Sherlock Holmes has been called in to investigate the death of two women at a country estate. It has all the markings of the Fenians. Players make up the clues that Holmes finds. Truth must be revealed.

This is an Engle Matrix Game so each player champions one of the characters of the story. Each turn they buy the right to make up the next clue discovered. This is not automatic though. The player picks another player to decide what their "to happen" roll is. Other players may jump in with counter-arguments forcing a big dice rolling contest. Players try to pin the murder on one another. It never plays the same way twice because people make up different clues each time.

The Fenian Murders uses the same rules as The Case of the Dead Duke. If you like murder mystery then you’ll love this game.

This is an intermediate level Matrix Game. It comes in a box with a 20x15 inch hard board map, character sheet book, rules book, scenario book, wooden counters, plastic gold coins and 2 dice.

TYPE: Intermediate Engle Matrix Game
PLAYERS: 2 to 10

COMPLEXITY: Moderate
REPLAYABILITY: High
COST: $40.00

Patients at Miskatonic State Hospital for the Insane receive state of the art therapy – 1923 state of the art! There is something going on here. The players take on the roles of doctors and patients. Each turn they make up clues that show what is actually going on. After a plot is uncovered they must stop it.

Most of the characters are mentally ill but this doesn’t stop them from helping. They were probably saving the world from Lovecraftian monsters before being committed anyway.

From the publisher's website:

HP Lovecraft wrote about the reanimation of corpses when my Flapper Grandmother was doing the Charleston with the boy in the band (Grand Dad). In this intermediate level game you take Lovecraft’s start and make your own story. First you find clues that reveal what is going on. Once the plot is outed you make a choice. Am I good or evil? Good guys try to stop plots, evil ones help them succeed. You never know whose side anyone is on till the end game.

If you like Horror Board Games you’ll love this game!

July 2009

Dead Man on Campus is an Engle Matrix Game that allows you to do real time story telling in a boardgame. Each player champions one of the characters at Miskatonic University in 1923. The game host reads an opening hook about how bodies have gone missing from the graveyard. At this point the players take over telling the tale. Each turn a player buys the right to make the next action in the game. They make up a short scene and pick another player to be their referee. The ref sets the "to happen" roll and the player rolls. It either happens and is written down by the players or doesn't. If players like they can buy the right to make a counter-argument to the first players action. A dice rolling contest settles what happens. As you see the rules are very simple but they allow a completely unique game every time.

Players quickly learn how to play - it only takes a turn or two and then never need to look at the rules again. It does take creativity though. If you don't like making stuff up then this is not the game for you but if you do like quick paced back and forth creation then you'll love it.

The boxed board game comes with a hard back board that is color and varnished but I must say that these are hand crafted by the game maker so this is more an art product than a slick made in China game. The game uses wooden counters with stickers but as you wish they can be replaced by metal figures. Cthulhu figures work great!

description: The pawn (the player who is courageous enough to cross  the robber woods) walks slowly through the forest. When it reaches a field that matches a card that the player has coins are dealt as a reward. The player who has the most valuable coins at the end wins the game.

boardgame: Pare los Facists! Battle for La Coruna Road: Jan.1937
description:
PARE LOS FACISTS (Stop the Facists) is a simulation on the critical battle also known as the Battle for the La Coruna Road that took place from Jan. 3 to 16th, 1937. In this particular phase of this Nationalist offensive (planned by the German advisers and their Blitzkrieg tech-nique) it was the first time the German Panzers met the Russian T26B tanks. Although their numbers were small both altered their doctrines because of them.

Game Scale: 
Each Day Game Turn represents one day of real time.
Each map hexagon represents 500 yds from side to side.
Each unit counter represents a battalion or company.
Tank units represent 4-5 tanks, each air-craft is a squadron, each AT unit is a battery of 4 guns.

Delicio, the yummy food game

Object of the game: 
The first player to fill his or her plate with a balanced meal wins.

How to play:
Spread all the pieces of "food" out on the table, facedown, around the central board.  Each player takes a "plate", which has 8 colored squares on it.

Roll the six-sided die to move around the circular central game board and stop on a colored square.  Turn over a card corresponding to that color and place it on your plate.

Each colored space corresponds to a food group.  Each food card is identified by a color next to the food.

Red: meat and other protein (e.g. beans)
Yellow: breads and cereals
Green: fruits and vegetables
Blue: dairy products

Plates have one red, one blue, two yellow, and three green squares.

Førstehjelpsspillet er et familiespill som kan spillers med opptil 6 deltakere. Deltakerne skal på tur i Norge, underveis må de løse oppgaver og utføre førstehjelp for å komme videre mot målet. På turen støter de på både behagelige og ubehagelige overraskelser. Riktig besvart førstehjelpsoppgave belønnes med en bit av "Røde Kors-puslespillet" - når en deltaker har puslet et komplett rødt kors må vedkommende inn til vårt hovedkontor i Oslo for å vinne.

boardgame: Schwerpunkt! Decline and Fall of the German Armour Weapon, East Front 1944-1945
description:
Schwerpunkt! is a tactical game of combat on the East Front in the last two years of World War 2.  Units are squad-sized, hexes represent about 100 meters across, and game turns are 60 to 120 seconds of real time.  Six small scenarios are provided.

The rules are short and succinct, with no real surprises from the standard igo/ugo practice, with one exception ... players alternate, not in taking their full turns, but in initiating combat until all combat is completed, and then in moving until all eligible units have moved.  This tends to slow the game down somewhat, but the benefit is the ability of the players to respond to threats in a more timely manner.

The components include four geomorphic map sheets, each 8.5"x11"; six pages of rules; and 166 uncut counters.  The production quality is low as the game, as well as the magazine in which it came (more on that in a moment) is photocopied.  Photocopied, remember, using 1977 technology.

The magazine is Volume 1 Number 1, and therefore Issue 1, of Combat!, the International Magazine of Conflict Simulations.  It's published by Wellington Wargames, also known as Swedish Games Productions, the brainchild of Fredrik Malmberg, who is himself a bit of a prodigal.  He began his wargame producing career in earnest when he was 14 years old (see his design 'Fight for Tobruk!')  Only 200 copies of The Combat issue 1 were printed.
With much finger skill all players try together to build a tower, as high as possible. The player who gets rid of his pawns first wins the game.

Each player has five lives (bananas).

The first player rolls two dice and say a number. The highest number is always the first.  For example: 4 and 5 is 54. The next throws are possible: 31 (lowest), 32, 41, 42, 43, 51, 52, 53, 54, 61, 62, 63, 64, 65, doubles 11, 22, 33, 44, 55, 66 and finally 21 (best throw). Whatever you say, the next player must throw a higher number than you said. You can bluff, and tell a smaller or a higher number. The next player must decide if he believes you or not. If you believe the number, you roll the dice again and gives a higher number. If you say that the player is bluffing, the bluffer must show the dice. If you're right loses the bluffer a banana. If the throw was right, you lose yourself a banana.
When a player said 21, the next player decides if he believes the throw or not. Believing does mean not to look the dice and you lose 1 banana. Are you saying that the thrower is bluffing, then check out the dice. The loser pays 2 bananas.

If during the game you shake the dice and you don’t like the throw, you can shake you again. You may not look that throw and you must give it blind to the other player; and you must say a higher number.
The player can also say "more than enough". If the dice shows a higher number, you can call a low throw. So the next player has the chance to say a higher number without looking at the dice. The poker cup goes quickly around the table, but it could be a bluff.

The winner is the player who’s left with one or more bananas.

The idea of the game is to collect as many musicians as possible. All musicians and the director must have found their places in the orchestra. Each player counts how many musicians he has collected. The player with the biggest orchestra wins the game.
The idea of the game is to guess the combination of the safe with as little tries as possible. After two rounds the winner is the player who cracked the code with the least guess tries.

In order to find hidden treasures in the palace, you must go   through many rooms, which are full of strange articles trying to find those treasures. With good memory and some luck the treasures come into sight.
The games' idea is to catch as many number cards as possible. But beware: The number cards may be collected only in the correct order.
The small dog gang is on a bone hunt. The player, who collected three bones with his dog first, wins the round.
The player, who finds the most jewels, becomes new Frog King. Whoever watches well and can memorize much, has a good chance finish the game sitting on the kings' throne.

From the rules:

Five dice spotted 2 to 6, with a fish replacing the 1 spot. the fish on the one spot is a Black Crappie.
The object is to catch the most number of fish.

Players have three rolls of the dice. The player rolls all five dice on his or her first roll, trying to produce a 6. If on the first roll the player gets a 6, the player sets that die aside and scores any fish thrown as part of his or her catch. The player then rolls the remaining four dice two additional times, trying to increase his or her catch of fish.

Each turn is played the same. A player must get a 6 before scoring the catch of fish. If a player does not roll a 6 during his or her three rolls per turn, the player cannot score fish on that turn, and passes the dice.

The player catching the most fish after each player has had three turns, is the winner.

Contains: 5 green fish dice

boardgame: Stop Light: The Amazing Magnetic Race Game
description:
Stop Light is the amazing magnetic maze game.  Magnetic traffic lights change like magic at any of 100 intersections.

There are two ways to play.  In the first a card is drawn and the first person to get their car to the location first wins that card.  Another card is then drawn and play continues in this fashion until all 18 cards have been collected.  The winner is the person with the most cards.  The alternate way to play is the Cross Town game.  The cards are not used in this one and the winner is first player to cross town by hitting the fewest red lights.

Magnets are placed in the plastic base according to the instructions and then the game board is placed on the base.  As the players car moves on the board the magnet in the bubble in the car may flip from red to green depending on the magnetic action caused by the base.

boardgame: Scrabble Junior: Dora the Explorer Edition
description:
Explore the magic of wordplay with Dora and her friends.  Play your very first game of scrabble with Dora and her friends.  Learn simple words in English and Spanish.  Teaches word building and letter recognition.  Two different games and two levels of play for both younger and older kids.  Features double sided board, double sided cards and auto-corrective tiles.

This edition of Scrabble Junior has a two-sided board, one for English words and one for Spanish words. The rules play similarly to other editions of Scrabble Junior. The goal is to lay down letter tiles one at a time to complete words pre-printed on the board. Letter tiles are played twice per turn, and must be used from the beginning of each word and sequentially toward the end. All tiles are face up once held by a player. Kids learn the strategy of maximizing point values for themselves while preventing points for other players. They also learn its okay if other players do earn points because of the rules that a letter must be played if a space is available to play it on. Players use all the tiles available to cover all of the spaces on the board.

AppLEOpoly is a promotional game published by the Leo Burnett Company, Inc to give to their employees on their 54th anniversary. The game is basically a Monopoly clone but with a few differences. For example, the house rule of collecting money when landing on Free Parking is an actual rule in AppLEOpoly. (The space is called "Free Coffee.")

The name comes from Leo Burnett Company's tradition of leaving Apples at the reception desk for visitors. Consequentially, the "Go" space on the board is called "Reception Desk" and is illustrated with a bowl of Apples." Other squares are accounts that the company holds. The "Jail" square is called "Working Weekend."

Greater Miamiopoly is a clone of Monopoly. The real estate squares are all advertisements for Miami businesses, sports teams, and media. This game used to be given away by the Miami Herald newspaper as a business present.

This is a game intended to teach children about dogs. The rulebook has a general information section on dogs, including what meaning of different behavior in dogs, what an angry/scared dog looks like, and how to teach children about dogs. Sometimes, the rules instruct the players to act dog like (barking, rolling etc.)

The game includes a board with an imaginary village printed on it, 4 playing pieces (dogs) on plastic bases, a deck of cards which represent 24 breeds of dog - some of which are "leaders" - and one 6-sided die. All of the dogs belong to families living in the village, identified by the first letter of a dog's name.

The game is subdivided into 6 different variants:

Game #1: 2-4 players, 10-15 minutes. This is described as a "warm-up" game, the board is not used. Players are "leader" dogs trying to gather their own pack by drawing cards blindly. The player with the most dogs after all non-leader breeds have been drawn wins.

This game also has a solitaire variant.

Game #2: 2-4 players, 30 minutes. Again, the players represent leaders trying to gather a pack. This is accomplished by visiting the 4 houses in the village to pick up dogs, then take them to the dog park.

This game has rule variants to make it a bit more challenging.

Game #3: 2-4 players, 20-30 minutes. Again, the players represent leaders, but this time they already have a pack. The players must return the dogs in their pack to the families, then find a way to their own house.

Game #4: 3 or more players, 10-20 minutes. This time the players must co-operate to gather a pack and take it to the dog park, playing against time (die rolls).

This game also has a solitaire variant.
 Game #5: 2-4 players, 20-30 minutes. This is basically a version of the Game of Authors - players are trying to complete series of dogs belonging to the same family.

Game #6: Solitaire, variable playing time. The aim is to rescue dogs from the kennel by pairing up the kennel dogs with free dogs.

Description from Uncles Games:

"Similar to Scrabble, players are creating word puzzles in a crossword fashion, only with Saygo, you do not have a community board. Instead, players builds their own word puzzles in front of them. Letters are worth points (vowels are worth 1 point, consonants 2 points, and super consonants J, K, Qu, X, and Z, are worth 4) the object of the game is to be the first player to reach 500 points. Every player randomly selects eight tiles and places them face down. Simultaneously, players turn them over and begin building the puzzles with as many letters from their individual pile as possible. Once someone has used up all eight tiles, he or she says "Saygo" and all players will select another tile. If nobody can use their tiles it is a stalemate, and everyone selects another tile. Should players find they can not use all of their tiles, they have the option of rearranging their puzzles as often as needed, in attempt to use all letters. The game continues until all tiles are used and the last person to use his/her last tile yells "done". Once the group verifies the accuracy of the winning player's words, the winner collects the remaining letters and adds them to his/her score. Once a player has 500 points (that number can be lowered or raised if desired), the round is finished and players can play again."

This is a children's game based on the fairy-tales by the Brothers Grimm.

It comes with a colorful board, 36 story tiles, 4 player pawns and a six-sided die.  The rulebook also contains the nine stories found in the game.

Players roll to move their pawns along the track, collecting sets of tiles.

From the box cover:

Märchenland - "Das Spiel mit 9 berühmten Märchen de Gebrüder Grimm"

And on the back of the box:

Die Spieledee
"Es war einmal ein Zauberwald.  Dort machten sich lustige kleine Zwerge auf den Weg nach 9 Märchen der Gebrüder Grimm zu suchen.  Seit vielen Jahren hält in dem Zauberwald eine Fee die Märchen in ihrem Bann.
Nun gilt es, diese 9 Märchen zu befreien.  Manchmal auch mit Unterstützung des Zauberstables.  Auf geht's ihr flinken Zwerge und huscht durch Zauberwald!"

boardgame: A Tour Through the British Colonies and Foreign Possessions
description:
A Tour Through The British Colonies and Foreign Possessions is from an original hand coloured lithographic game published in about 1850 by John Betts of London. The game is a whistle-stop tour around the world from London via Africa, India, Japan, the West Indies, the United States of America, Canada, and finally, a race back to London. The first part of the journey includes the choice of two routes. The overland journey visits the city of Alexandria and the sea cruise calls in at Sierra Leone and the Cape.

With 36 stops along the route, the visitor participates in a remarkable, colonial experience of the world.

The game includes a box, mounted color map, reproduction rule book and catalog, The Historical Games Collection catalog, four re-created pawns lightly tipped in colors, and a six sided teetotum (spinner - dice were considered at the time as something used for gambling).

Game play involves spinning, moving your pawn the appropriate number of spaces, and then reading the educational paragraph about the colony or possession you have landed on.

The reproduction edition's rulebook notes: "These rules are copied directly from the originals and reflect the moral and social outlook of early 19th century imperialist peoples. The facts and attitudes are often wrong, intolerant and patronizing and should be read from a historical perspective."

boardgame: The Noble Game of Elephant and Castle or Travelling in Asia
description:
The Noble Game of Elephant and Castle was first published in 1822 as a hand coloured engraving by William Darton of London.

The game is played on a magnificent board depicting an elephant surmounted by a howdah (ornate carriage) with an Indian mahout (driver). The mysteries of the Orient are discovered through a grand tour from the Frozen Ocean in the Arctic wastes of north Russia thence south through Asia into India, China, and Japan. The game was published at the height of the British Empire and outrageously describes the appearance, customs and culture of the region's peoples, comparing their way of life to that of the British.

The game comes in a box with a mounted full color reproduction board, reproduction rules, Catalog of The Historical Games Collection, four "travelers" (pawns) - each lightly tipped with a different color, 16 counters (chips, each with the rose face lightly colored to match pawns), and a teetotum (top with eight sides, numbered 1-8 - dice being items of gambling).

Each player spins and moves their pawn a number of spaces, attempting to progress to 25 by exact count. Each space has a description in the accompanying rule book to be read aloud, describing the scene from an area of Asia.  The counters are used to mark off missed turns assigned by the descriptions.

The rules note: "These rules are copied directly form the originals and reflect the moral and social outlook of early 19th century imperialist peoples. The facts and attitudes are often wrong, intolerant and patronizing and should be read from a historical perspective."

From the publisher:

You asked for it (didn't you?), and now it's here (well, almost). Packing with forty professionally printed, and glaringly-glossy pages (including cover) and filled with World at War wisdom, the World at War: Gamer's Guide is on pre-order. You'll find a preview of the upcoming World at War boxed game, World at War: Blood and Bridges. Two new scenarios, Clearing Hell's Highway, and Poor Bloody Infantry.  Two battle reports with semi-fictionalized accounts recounts of WaW scenarios (Some of you call these after action reports). A ten page article examining strategy in the World at War series and the specific tactics needed to win all six Eisenbach Gap scenarios.  Even variants for night fighting, and a design your own scenarios "how to" article. And much more... including a unit-by-unit analysis of the platoons of Eisenbach Gap...

World at War has been a pleasant, if mild, surprise for Lock ‘n Load Publishing. I designed the game because I wanted to play it. Yes, in the back of my mind, I felt that there might be a market for something depicting the war that never happened, but primarily I created the game because it interests me.

In ten years of writing and designing, that’s the only thing I’ve found that works. I’ve designed and written on spec, and gave those pieces my best shot, but it seems that the games, the writing, that gets my juices flowing are the things other people also enjoy.

I absolutely love playing World at War. I like the combat system, am intrigued by the era, and fascinated by the idea of a series of games that link together to tell a story. So, I’m happy that other people enjoy it too.

I also hope that this guide enhances your enjoyment. We have strived to make a guide that is visually appealing, interesting, and diversified. Inside you’ll find articles on strategy, the percentages of rolling dice in World at War, battle reports (or after action reports as some of you prefer), and unit analysis. What you won’t find are dry as dust articles on the actual forces that lined the West German border or mind-numbing diatribes on Soviet Doctrine. Why? Because that stuff just doesn’t interest me.

There are lots of tables comparing the strength and weakness of an M1 Abrams platoon and a T-72 platoon in the World at War system. And we go through the platoons of Eisenbach Gap, unit-by-unit, and even provide a walk through for winning Blind Sided (a Death of the 1st Panzer scenario.)

All this in a beautifully laid out, two-color 40-page glossy magazine. Yeah, this one's printed. You can hold it in your hands.

Not a stand-alone game.

Expands:


    World at War: Eisenbach Gap
    World at War: Death of the 1st Panzer

Players contribute to defeat the evil dragon Nesselbrand (Nettle) in this tactical movement game. How much did you contribute at game end, when the bad dragon is defeated?

Now five to six players jump in on the new adventures taking place on the island of Catan. The expansion includes new scenarios and opens up roads to even greater wealth or bitter poverty for up to six players.

A free web-published card game based on GMT's SPQR. Each player uses a deck composed of cards representing ancient military units (infantry, cavalry) and special commands (flank, rally). Cards are drawn from these decks and played on a board representing the battlefield. The board is divided into three zones, the center and two wings. Units on the field may move, missile fire and shock with the enemy.

The game features a variety of historical armies and commanders, as well as fantasy forces.

description:
A set of rules which can be played on any map showing political subdivisions. The players take turns placing one piece at a time on the mapboard in order to capture territories. Pieces may not be placed into enemy held spaces, and a player may not have more than three pieces in a single space. The diceless combat system requires you to establish a larger force in spaces adjacent to those occupied by the enemy. The game continues until both players pass, at which time the victor is the player who controls the most spaces.

The game was inspired by the 1983 SSI computer game Fortress, which was played on a 6x6 grid and limited to 21 turns.

Learn the geography and customs of the people in this African adventure by all-terrain vehicle, crossing the different landscapes of the continent and living the most unexpected dangers.

It exalts the competitive spirit and capacity of planning to raise every race.

In the spring time as the sun rises and toads go on a journey,   traffic becomes dangerous for them. In this game the toads walk on the motorway. We must save them with the help of the mole. There are tunnels under the motorway. If we are able to get all of the toads into a tunnel we win.

165 cards are mixed. The left neighbour always asks the question. On each card side a question is printed with three wrong answers. That is the joke of the thing, and  this means for the players: listen exactly and watch out! With every correct answer there is a banana as reward. If an ape on the board is completely equipped with 6 bananas, the player who has that ape wins.

Monopoly-like game with versions of different cities.

Players roll two dice. A red die and a white one. Players roll and move their pawns along the local different companies in a city. When a player lands on a company, he/she has to buy the property or pay the owner rent. If a player has a set of companies of the same color the rent rises.
Each turn a player has to take a card based on the roll of the red die. The game ends when one player owns all property.

A free promotional game from the chips brand Croky, combining roll-and-move with memory.

Each player plays a different parrot (that stands for one the the tastes).

Small travel basketball game.  3 standard dice (1 white "shooting" die, 2 blue defensive dice), and 2 red offensive dice with roman numerals 2-7. 1 red pawn to mark ball position. 1 dry erase marker to keep score  on "board", 1 plastic coated card with image of basketball court.

Rolls of the dice determine court position, turnovers, blocked shots, rebounding, steals, fouls, and scoring.

In this game, players race to move four pawns from their starting positions around the board into their "Home Country."  Basic gameplay resembles Pachisi or Sorry!, but there are a couple of twists.  First of all, movement is controlled by a deck of standard playing cards (with some extra Aces and Kings) -- most cards move a pawn forward by their face value, but some have special powers (get a pawn out of the starting area, move multiple pawns, trade places, move backwards, etc.).  Additionally, while the game can be played as a free-for-all, it is also possible for players to split into partnerships and aid their partners in the race to victory.

The game is subtitled "An International Card Game," featuring rules in eight different languages and decorating its board with the flags of many of the countries where it can be played.

Each challenge contains a country map with 5 pieces of information about the country:

     Country's currency

"Stonewall" Jackson's Shenandoah Valley Campaign consists of a set of divisional level miniature wargame rules and a highly political campaign system. The players all play on the Union side, in the positions of Banks and his divisional commanders.

The campaign system is designed to generate battles, either by player initiative or automated Southern attacks. The players choose each turn to train their troops, administer conquered areas, indulge in 'politicking' in Washington, write or execute orders, request reinforcements or send them to McLellan's army in the Peninsula. If no Union forces contact the Confederate forces, the latter will make contact.

The miniature combat rules are aimed to portray small battles 2-6 brigades on each side) in a short game. They emphasize terrain, reserves, command ability and troop quality.

description:
(Description taken from their homepage):

The pyramids of Egypt are among the most enduring and mysterious symbols of the ancient Egyptian civilization. The pyramids are considered to be one of the seven wonders of the world. The leaders of this ancient time-the pharaohs were clever strategists who would use all manner of tactics to ensure that their dynasties remained in power.

Two powerful symbols of ancient Egypt; the pyramid and the scarab are combined to create a unique game that will challenge you to think like the pharaohs.

“Battle of the Pyramids” is a strategic game for two players that combine the principles of planning, strategic thinking, memory and surprise.

Not for the faint hearted Battle of the Pyramids challenges you to be the first to have your pyramids reach the other-side unscathed.  Conceal the scarabs within your pyramids to impede your opponent’s assault. Plan your strategy and attempt to get your opponent to reveal their pyramids hidden secrets.

Ship descriptions and record sheets, plus campaign rules for Voidstriker.

The Voidstriker Ship Recognition Guide #1 details 16 ships from the Terran Defense Force, the Martian Republican Guard, and the Jovian Alliance Navy. In addition, record sheets are included for all the sample ships in the Voidstriker rulebook, and a simple set of campaign rules allows you to take your place among the stars. By force, if necessary...

    Voidstriker

Something has gone horribly wrong.  The Great Elemental War has been raging on for over 100 years.  The Flux is in tatters, and the Creationists can barely keep it together.  As the 5 Elementals sects continue to wage their war, an unholy power has slowly been breaking down it's cage.

The Harmonists and Elitists combined research of their perverse magic has lead to a terrifying discovery.  Directly tapping the power of Chaos, they have gained tremendous power.  What they didn't realize is that the more they exploited this power, the easier it had becoming for the Gods of Chaotica and their cultists to escape their long forgotten prisons.

The Chaos has been unleashed.  The very existence of mankind is now in danger.  Can these ancient Gods be stopped, or is their power too much to overcome?

Chaotica and Cultists is a free expansion for Elementalis. This expansion allows the game to handle up to 5 players with two new Mage types to diversify game play.

From publishers website:

"You are invited to the party! This memory-matching game will engage children with a charming array of colorful and crazy hats. Pick a pair, build a stack and win the game! Hat Stackers Party includes 11 X 17" double-sided folding magnetic game board, 40 chunky foam magnet game pieces, and full-color illustrated instruction booklet with easy-to-follow rules."

Syl-la-blesis the game that encourages you to think big. Choose a bigger word to get more points, but spell it right or you score zero! "Thesaurus" and "Spelling Bee" cards test your vocabulary and spelling skills. This is a unique game for playing with varied ages, because you set your own point goals--for individuals or the group--before you start the game. Tested by the Canadian Toy Testing Council and recipient of the 2007 Seal of Approval from The National Parenting Center.

"Be quick because each player only has one minute to “play on words” and win the challenge! Set the timer and, before it sounds, write as many connecting words as possible using only the letters on the eight cubes shown. The more words “played on” and the more letters used, the higher the score. Challenge friends or yourself with the solitaire version. Play On Words includes a built-in electronic timer. "

24 cards are distributed evenly to all fellow players.
The player with the “start” - card puts these on the table and begins the round, by pulling a card of any fellow player. Afterwards this Turn player is now to pull with a fellow player etc.
If he pulls a point card, he puts it wordless to its other cards. He must show an action card and had to fulfill  it immediately. As soon as a player pulls the “stop” - card, the round is terminated, and everyone recounts the points, which he holds straight on the hand.

(Translated from the Japanese rulebook)
In this game, the players are owners of inns, and the goal is to get as many staying guests, earning the most money in a set period of time.
But it's not easy preparing rooms for all those people who have come to the festival in town. Among the normal guests like travelers and merchants, there is the King, the General and even assassins and Demon Lord! It's a chaotic mix of everything.
Each guest has their own condition for staying the night and paying the room. Some are content if you serve them tasty food, some look only for good service and some are looking for a good fight…
If you can't please the guest, not only will you not get paid, but you have to pay instead, so you'll have to be careful which guests you accommodate. You can push dangerous guests to your neighbour's door, but if you're not careful, you might get one yourself!
The character the players play all have different areas they are good at and bad at. And they, too, are former adventurers, so they have individual skills to attract or deter guests. The one who uses these skills the best, gets the most content customers and earns the most money during the festival will be declared the winner.

(Translated from the Japanese rule book)
In this game, players cooperate in order to defeat huge monsters. All damage dealt to the monster is directly converted into Victory Points, and the one with the most VPs when the third monster is defeated, is declared the winner.
But it is not an easy task to deal damage to these huge creatures. If you're too far away, you can't deal any damage at all. In order to deal damage, and earn points, you need to get close to the monster, and run the risk of taking damage yourself.
And dealing damage isn't the only thing you must be careful about. The other players are aiming at your back. They might try to push you to the front line. If you're pushed to the front, and the monster gets you, all your points are halved!
Seeing to that the leading player gets nicely defeated, and still having cards left to deal some damage to the monster, will make you the winner.

(Translated from the Japanese rulebook):
The goal of this game is to collect the most money for your marriage. But it's not just any money. It's the treasure of the dragon that you (think you) defeated.
As the players enter the dragon's lair and start collecting treasure, the dragon will eventually awaken. The players must then run away from the maddened dragon, while holding onto as much treasure as they can. But if they have a lot of treasure, they'll be slowed down. If the dragon gets them, not only will they get hurt, but they'll also drop the treasures, one after the other! So stack you items carefully, equip and re-equip, trip the other player's legs as much as you can and run for your life.
The player who gets out with the most treasure is declared the winner

Link your stones to gain points. 
The object of the game is to gain the most points by forming links between your stones. Players take turns placing a stone. If the stone can be linked to any other friendly stone(s) in one of the eight directions (north, south, east, west, northeast, northwest, southeast, southwest), then a line of friendly pebbles is placed on all spaces along the paths connecting the stones. A connection cannot be made if an enemy stone lies along the path to the friendly stone.

The score corresponds to the number of pebbles owned by each player. The game ends when there are no remaining moves. The player with the most pebbles wins.

You don't have to wait till the stars are right! If you want to summon your favorite Mythos creature, make the stars OK yourself!

The players first create their own star diagram on the piece of paper and then play various cards to recreate it on the board.

Good clean fun for the whole Whateley family.

The emperor and its royal household walk proudly in the palace garden. Everyone made the cunning weavers manufacture a marvelous dress for him. By great fear to be considered as too stupid neither the emperor nor its officials dare to throw a view on their shade. There they can recognize that they are naked in reality. Fortunately a small boy, who sits in a tree in the palace garden, and the players don't let themselves deceive by the stupid trick of the weavers. A little to search they must thereby, and the cunning weavers try likewise to donate confusion. Who finds three of the twelve naked images first, exposed the treacherous weavers and wins the game.

A fascinating game of skill and chance playing two of everyone's favorite card games-cribbage and poker. Where the players decide whether to play out their hand as a cribbage game or slip into high stakes poker.

The object of the game is collect the most chips in a variety of ways. By means of green Cribble cards. By strategic use of the Blue Cribble cards. By avoiding penalty cards and red pegs holes. By pegging around the Cribble track to be the first to reach the last hole and collect the Cribbage pot. And by call Cribble to play high-stakes poker for individual poker pots.

boardgame: Reader's Digest National Word Power Challenge
description:
The box that I received is "Set 1" in the official card game series. It contains three decks of 64 cards each: Over the Rainbow, Speaking Out, and Lost in Space.

Each deck is a themed game of Go Fish containing 16 sets of 4 cards each. However, the cards contain advanced vocabulary words from Reader's Digest Word Power columns. The four facets of each card are: Definition and Part of Speech, Word Origin, Synonyms, Fill in the Blank, the latter of which is an example sentence in which a blank indicates where the word would go.

On your turn, you ask for a card whose [insert facet] is [insert facet's description], e.g. "I would like a card whose synonyms are abandon, abdicate, discard, throw away." If the player has a "Jettison" card, he gives it up. The rest of the rules are Go Fish.

The box includes a supplementary color guide with pictures and further explanations for each word.

boardgame: Salvanèl: flight from the enchanted wood
description:
Who is the Salvanèl?
The Salvanèl is a goblin that frequently appears in the legends of Trentino, in northern Italy. Until not many decades ago, in every valley you could find someone who said they had seen the Salvanèl, even though actually encountering him was extremely unlikely.
They described him as a small man, boyish-looking, and always with some red feature on him: a hat, clothes or hair.

This time the Salvanèl, running to and fro, has left his magical tracks everywhere. Anyone who treads on them by mistake is bound to get lost - and after much wandering, finds himself back where he started. Now you are lost in the wood too!
To get back home you must make three good moves to reconstruct a path that will take you out of the wood. But the goblin will also help you, if you meet him!

At the beginning of every turn, the guide picks a colored card. This card has two effects:
1-The Salvanèl immediately moves toward the border indicated by the card, and if he meets a player... He immediately gains an extra turn!
2-The player indicated by the card plays his turn... And if he meets the Salvanèl... He plays again!

In every turn, players can use one of these moves:
1-Spin:choose a tile and rotate it in any direction you like
2-Puff of Wind: move one or more tiles of a horizontal or vertical line
3-Jump: move your token from the tile where it is to another one next to it.

The first player who jumps out of any tile on the side of his colour wins!

This game covers the Battle for Berlin, and is designed by the same game designer of The Battle for Iwo Jima. The game includes one A2-sized map of Berlin, divided into areas in a manner similar to Storm Over Arnhem, as well as one A3-sized reproduction of the map. This is similar in nature to the one featured at the back of the rulebook in Storm Over Arnhem, and presumably to be used for the same purpose. The game also includes 81 counters.

This game was designed by Works-Zero (www.works-zero.com).

Tactical-level game with three scenarios, each with a map.

Each player tries to eat full with cards of food points so that it can oversleep the winter in its hiding place. Who gets over the “winter sleep” without starving first, wins.

It's the world of the Vietnam War seen through different eyes, the eyes of the men of special units that fought a different kind of war. They conducted unorthodox operations, such as reconnaissance, sabotage, prisoner snatches, rescue of downed airmen, ambushing the enemy, and in many cases out guerrilla-ing   the guerrillas.

These missions were almost always carried out on the enemy's home turf, with the odds stacked against the interlopers. FNG: Unconventional Warfare is all about these men and the deadly missions they went on.

FNG: Unconventional Warfare (UW for short) UW introduces the "tactical role-playing" side of FNG. UW blends the traditional role playing game with a fast and easy combat system designed for a more personal level. Combining these two aspects allows players to enjoy the cinematic aspect of role-playing while keeping the excitement of a miniatures wargame.

UW has a unique campaign system that generates your Missions and links them together in a simple yet realistic way. UW also features a character advancement system that allows you to "personalize" your troops.

And like all Two Hour Wargames rules they're designed to be played with any figures you already have and in any scale! Play solo, play on the same side, or play head-to-head it�s up to you! So pull out those favorite figures, pop in your favorite 60's CD, and remember... JUST PLAY THE GAME!

From the One Page Wars game series.

"RftE attempts to simulate the conflict between rebels revolting and attempting to take control of their nation from a foreign power. In this fantasy banana republic the nation of Britain has held control for the past 115 years. Now the rebels have started to advance from the north towards the major hub of Port Hanley."

This small format game is played on a narrow L-shaped map. Victory goes to the player who controls most of the seven cities on the board after twenty turns. The British units include Royal Marines, Light Infantry, Challenger tanks, and Puma helicopters.

Content:
48 picture cards

As time runs all players try to fit cards in their hands at the same time. It is important to catch the cards as fast as lightning. The winner is the player with the most caught cards.

Spion im Werk or Spy in the facory was produced by Adam Opel AG in order to encourage the employees to communicate more with each other. The Goal of the game is to catch a spy, that has sneaked off into the Opel factory in Rüsselsheim, Germany. There are two teams: One plays the spy and the others play  a team trying to catch the spy, but the spy has some sneaky skills to avoid being caught by the other team,  If he succeeds in stealling a specific amount of secret files from the factory, the spy wins.

The game mechanic is a mixture of roll & move and answering questions.

This unique variation on Backgammon called Zarsiz Tavla in Turkish has no luck to it.  It uses a unique system of players alternating turns picking one die facing, and their opponent picking the second, in order to determine moves. The end result is a player is left with a die facing they picked, and the other their opponent picked.  Players are allowed to use the die they picked twice, as a roll, several times during the game to act as a rolling doubles.

Full Information on the game can be obtained here:
http://zarsiztavla.gop.edu.tr/index-e.htm

The rules are under how to play.

Each of the two or 4 players chooses a color and puts his 5 pawns on the start fields of the same color. The point is to bring the own pawns across the board to the opposite finish fields. The player who is there with his 5 pawns wins the game.
A quiz-game from the fifties - the player gets the question card.   Either the player who is asked, the questioning player or the referee can answer it.

Pay attention to your own moves - as well as to the move of your co-players. Because only the player who is able to make an error free move at last can win.
The difficulty consists of the fact that no pawn of the same color may stand left, right, above, down or diagonally beside the set cone.
Ab, durch die Mitte  is an easily understandable tactics game for 2 or 4 people, where the point is, to be the first to have their pawns disappear through the hole in the center of the game box.

A trivia game based on the traffic safety campaign "Ich Fahr' Fair" (roughly: "I drive considerately").  Players earn puzzle pieces by answering multiple-choice, traffic trivia questions in six categories.  Correct answers allow a player to roll again and continue their turn.

Contents; Game board, 6 wooden car pawns, question cards with answers, 6 puzzles with 6 pieces, rules and a dice.  
Note; the game is only available in a German language version.

Basic Tic-Tac-Toe only you must correctly answer a Bible trivia question--which category (out of nine choices) is listed on a movable/variable chart under the tic-tac-toe board--for the space you want to occupy.

Clever board/component setup makes it a convenient game to play.

This two-player strategy game is based on trying to cross the board with your pieces to get into a goal area, removing it.  The first player to get 3 of his pieces into the goal wins.

The unusual mechanic to this game is that you can move any piece on your turn, yours or your opponent's, any unblocked distance on a line.  If you move your pieces, the end space must have a different number of adjacent pieces from the space where it started, while if you move an opposing piece, it must end adjacent to the same number of pieces.

A simple board game, similar to Sorry! or Parchisi.  Movement takes place with a roll of the dice.
The game comes in two phases.  First, players progress through "Desert Shield", in which they collect cards that can later be used to defeat threats.  Second, they move to "Desert Storm", in which they use their cards to overcome obstacles on the board.  When a player reaches the middle of the board, they have won the game and liberated Kuwait.  
If you encounter an obstacle that there is no card for, then you must restart Desert Storm.  If this happens three times in a row, then Desert Shield must be repeated as well.  There are also locations on the board that will send you back to the start.

Dresda is a ‘fast-play’ fantasy battle skirmish system designed for small numbers of troops yet involving more than a little role-playing on the part of the players. Indeed, unlike many conventional wargames with little or no ‘role-playing’ element, in Dresda you are your units' Commander in the field, and you not only have the responsibility of commanding the unit in battle, but also the headaches of paying your troops, buying their equipment and generally trying to improve your (and their) financial position.

Dresda is a scenario driven game, not points based one. This means that careful thought needs to be given to the designing of scenarios by the GM. So, although you can line up your troops on opposite sides of the table and simply advance until you bump into each other in the centre, you'll find this isn't really much fun, and you'll quickly find that your games will be much more enjoyable if you (and/or a good GM) put some effort into designing an interesting or challenging scenario.

Of course, it also helps to create a feeling of unit continuity by treating your unit as a group of individuals rather than disposable 'cannon (or halberd)-fodder'. Name each of them, decide what they like to eat, drink and how they feel about their Mum, and suddenly you can see them as characters in a role-playing game, not just as troops in a ‘one-off’ wargame.

Over a number of games you'll see your unit develop, watch their fortunes wax and wane and individual characters come and go. Eventually you'll find that some of these factors will start to dictate your actions on the battlefield, for instance you may see ‘heroic’ traits develop in one character or cowardly traits in another as the miniatures begin to become characters in their own right, and not just anonymous lumps of painted white metal.

The Aim of the Game

The basic aim of Dresda is to raise a small unit of fighters, fight battles and get rich!

Unfortunately, whilst this basic aim is simple, it isn't necessarily easy. What's more, this is likely to be a more 'hands-on' experience than you may be used to, even in skirmish wargaming. That's because you, the Player, will take on the role of your unit's Commander, and will be represented on the battlefield by a miniature figure!

From the instructions: Fetch the Ink! was devised to give an entertaining insight into printshop routine, which can be pretty hectic at times! In view of the tight delivery deadlines involved there is often a fine line between human error and superhuman achievement.

"Ink is a basic essential for printing, so the winner of the game is the player whose team is the first to reach the coveted ink buckets in the centre of the board."

Each player starts off at the printing press in the colored corresponding corner and strives to reach the matching ink bucket in the middle of the board.

There are two circuits linked by four cross paths and a player must try to reach the colour spectrum via the line of squares in his or her colour.

Clubhouse Golf Dice Game loosely simulates a round of golf through the same scoring system - the lower the number of strokes, the better your score. The game comes with 5 dice: A white Tee die, a light green Fairway (F) die, a blue Water (W) die, a yellow Sand (S) die and a dark green putting (G for Green) die. Except for the putting die, the dice have letters on them indicating the next die to be cast. The putting die has numbers on it.

Each hole, a player starts by rolling the Tee die (the first stroke). Subsequently, the player rolls the next die indicated by the previous die, adding one stroke to their score for each die rolled. However, when the Water die is rolled, the player adds 2 strokes instead. When the Green (G) is rolled, the player rolls the putting die and adds the number rolled to his/her score - the hole then ends for that player and his/her score is the number of strokes played. Players can play 18 holes for a full round of golf. The lowest total score wins.

It's easier to get a hole-in-one in this game than in real golf - roll 'G' on the Tee die, followed by a roll of '0' on the putting die.

In this set collection game, players make circuits of the kitchen on the playing board, picking up ingredients (cards) from the various collection points (Larder, Garden, Kitchen Cupboard, Cellar and Back Door) in an attempt to be the first to make a finished meal (a main course and either a starter or pudding).

Each main course has 12 items to collect, each starter and pudding has 6, and the various collection points supply different types of items (e.g. bacon and butter from the fridge in the Larder, herbs and vegetables from the Garden, alcohol from the Cellar).

In addition to these, the board has several "lose a turn" spaces (some of which can be avoided by assistance found through answering the Front Door, e.g. a visiting niece who will help with the dishes), as well as two Telephone squares. Using these, players can challenge each other to hand over required items for the meal they are trying to compose.

At the end, points are scored for complete courses, any bonus items collected to go with the meal (e.g. flowers, or After Eight mints), and players may even try to convince the others that combinations of currently unused cards from their hands produce viable (but lower scoring) meals - the other players vote on whether such suggestions are acceptable. Any completely unused cards negatively affect the final scorings.
As many quiz-questions as possible have to be answered correctly, in order to reach the finish by correct answers and a good portion of luck. The winner may call himself "Pfiffikus".

A system of simple map, movement, and command rules designed to be used with any skirmish miniatures rules.

The designer says: "FGS is a simple system to be used as a support for skirmish miniature rules. The idea is quite simple: to provide a mechanism to make it possible to move your figures without any ruler while keeping the special 'feeling' of the 3-dimensional terrain."

"FGS is composed of two parts: the Movement System and the Command System; both systems can be used independently: the Movement System can be easily introduced on every existing skirmish rule to allow an easy management of the figures on the 3-dimensional map. The Command System can be used to create non-deterministic sequences of play."

boardgame: The Road to Berlin: The Last Act in the East
description:
Work continues on this long-awaited design by Joseph Youst. The current development team is moving to polish this operational simulation of the most intense four months of combat in the history of War.

German Army Groups keep supplies and the Luftwaffe's air power moving to support their subordinate armies holding the threatened bulwark in the East. Soviet Fronts keep supplies and the Red Falcons flooding west with the armies heading toward the target of Stalin's vengeance: BERLIN.

THE ROAD TO BERLIN: The Last Act in the East contains:

three Joseph Youst maps - one 22 x 34 inches, one 22 x 42 inches, one 34 x 44 inches - covering Europe from Courland to Hungary, and from the Western border of the Soviet Union to central Germany

VISTULA '45: Germany's Last Offensive - This introductory scenario combines all of the combat interactions in this system in the struggle for the town of Stargard, where the German Army expended its utmost efforts to hold back the enemy. Historically, this fight caused considerable concern in the Kremlin and allowed thousands of civilians to escape to the west. [Note: VISTULA '45: will also be available separately in Folio format.]

Simple roll-and-move game of traveling in Africa. The game consists of a cardboard folder w/an 8-fold game board paper map attached. No tokens or dice are provided.

The game consists of a series of events, triggered by landing on their respective space. The event legends are described in simple rhymes, and tie in with the board illustrations. They can be beneficial as well as bad. Players race to be first to #100.

Based on the popular book series by Eileen Christelow.

Objective: to be the first player to hold a hand of plus cards that add up to one dollar without having any minus cards.

Players are dealt a hand of five cards.  Players draw one card from draw pile. Minus cards may be discarded by discarding a plus card of the same value at the same time.  If there are no minus cards in a player's hand, play moves to the next player.  Play continues, with the discard pile being shuffled to form a new draw pile, until one player reaches the objective.

Geomancy is the practice of divination through tossing dirt. Perhaps in the same fashion, we can learn more about the world around us by tossing plastic pyramids. Through playing, the subject learns to communicate with the plastic pyramids within.

Geomancy is an Icehouse game played with one stash per player and a chessboard.

Geomancy is played in three rounds. In the first round, players take turns casting all of their small stones. The second round is all about casting medium stones. Finally, the game is concluded by casting out the large stones. While some players are recasting, other players are reorienting their pyramids. The goal is to cast and orient in a way that you can capture the other players stones. Points are awarded for your stones on the field at the end of the game along with the stones you have captured.

Complete rules can be found at the Icehouse Games Wiki.

description: The object of The Old Shell Game is to be the first player to get his/her shell with the hidden pea to the opposite side of the board. Four player games can be played as individuals or as partners. Each player receives 3 shells and only one pea. The players secretly shuffle their shells, and places them on the three squares of their color. Players take turns. Two dice are rolled, and one shell is moved the number of one dice and another shell is moved the number of the other dice. A challenge occurs if one player's shell lands on the same space as an opponent's shell. The challenged player must lift up his shell, and if the shell contains the pea, then the challenger loses. The challenged player loses if no pea is uncovered. The following points are added to each player's score: 20 points for reaching the finish with your shell that has the pea; 10 points for a successful challenge; and 5 points for successfully defending a challenge. Several rounds are played until a player or team reaches a score of 100 points.

Original Polish board game about Football Cup of Europe. Includes 32 teams which won National Championships in 1983/4 season. They are divided in three groups of power.

In the game are six original dice. They are used to draw a result of matches. Teams from the most powerful group use the best brown (0,1,3,3,4,4) and red (0,1,2,2,4,5) dice. The second group uses yellow (0,1,2,2,4,4) and green (0,1,1,2,3,5) dice. The worst group uses blue (0,1,1,2,3,4) and black (0,0,1,1,3,5) dice.

The cards are thoroughly shuffled and a dealer deals each player 4 cards.  Remaining cards are placed in the centre of the table.  The object in Piggy is to collect all four cards the same.  The first player decides on a card they don;t want and pass it to the player on their left.  Play continues, with players deciding on whether to draw a new card from the deck or pass one of their own.  Once a player has collected their set of 4, they stick out their tongue!  As other players notice, they follow suit.  The last person to notice receives a letter token P, I, G, G, or Y.  The winner is the last person in, with the least number of letters.  
Shorter versions can be played by just playing to P, I, G or deciding on how many rounds to play before starting.

Breakthrough is an icehouse game designed to be played on a chess board with 2 players, each needing 1 stash. The objective is to be the first player to move one of his pieces to the opposite end of the board.

Each player sets up his pyramids as desired on their back 2 rows of squares.

On a player's turn, he may move one pyramid one space.

i. Small pyramids may move and capture diagonally forward.

ii. Medium pyramids may move straight forward or diagonally forward, and they capture diagonally forward.

iii. Large pyramids may move straight forward, straight backward or diagonally forward, and they capture diagonally forward.

c. Captured pyramids are removed from play

Dominew is a domino-like game in which the tiles are made of plastics crosses with 2, 3 or 4 numbers on it. You score points by placing the tiles so that there are several similar numbers in a row (for example, 6 + 6 + 6 = 18 points) or as consecutive numbers (eg 2 + 3 + 4 = 9 points).

Stora skandiska kriget is a traditional hex-and-counter magazine game that came with the Swedish fanzine "Den fjättrade gåsen" #6, February 2004.

The game includes a cardboard map, size A4, an OOB, also A4, and a set of uncut laser printed counters. The magazine itself includes the rules and is of A5 size.

There are two scenarios, named "Vasakriget" & "Operation kuling" with two different countries & thus sets of units, both utilizing the same map. Both scenarios are set in the world of the Swedish post-apocalyptic RPG Mutant 2, of 1980:ies vintage.

Isle at war is game of conquest set on a fictitious island of warring feudal kingdoms, players tax their population and use the wealth to raise great armies and build castles. To win a player must conquer the entire island. Isle at war is a print and play game and can be printed out on one page, all that is required to play on top of that is a single 6 sided die.

A science fiction miniatures wargame with very streamlined rules to enable large scale battles to be played in reasonable periods of time.

The rules allow for a whole range of unit types from basic flak-jacket and assault rifle infantry, up through power-armoured infantry, tracked/wheeled/hover AFVs, WIGE (ground effect) units, enormous OGRE-style nuclear powered cybertanks and all kinds of mech (akin to Battletech, Robotech etc.) units - two, three four and six-legged are all accounted for.

The rules are for 1/300th scale units, enabling players to easily assemble an army from a variety of sources and manufacturers including Battletech figures, Epic WH40K figures, Ground Zero and a plethora of small white-metal figure manufacturers.

Included are outlines of the eight major power-blocs in the future world of OHMU, each with their own particular "flavour"  - from the ultra high-tech Pacific Rim Coalition to the more direct approach of the Russian Commonwealth; however all the army lists are very well balanced having been extensively playtested.

Although nominally a 2-player wargame the mechanics do easily permit more players to participate either as sub-commanders on two opposing sides, or even as a three-way (or more if you're ambitious) battle.

A very effective command & control framework is a key element of the game mechanics.  Another innovative concept, far ahead of its time, is the inclusion of scenarios that players bid on, attempting to undercut each other's force values.  The scenarios (if one opts to use them) themselves provide variety to the battles thus avoiding the typical "line up two equal armies and attack" situation.

96 page softcover A4 rulebook.  Black & white illustrations throughout, with 8 colour plates.

(And in case you were wondering, OHMU stands for Oversized Heavy Mechanized Unit...!)

Rex was published by "Rex-Verlag Rudolf Schilling" in Frankfurt am Main, Germany in 1900. It can be described as a mixture between Salta from 1899, since the pieces of each player have to cross the board to the other side, and Chess, for the pieces have different value and represent General, Horse, Soldier etc.

Unlike Chess, captured pieces are of a certain value (between 1 and 40!), and pieces all move in the same way (one step along the lines) crossing a grid of diagonally linked points in alternating dark and light rows. To start, pieces can jump from the "encampment" onto each of the points in the first row. Only pieces on dark points can be captured - obviously only by pieces on light points. After crossing the "battlefield", they "jump off" into the opponent`s encampment, where they are safe.
The game ends, if one player cannot do a legal move anymore. The player with less lost points wins the game. But aware: Not only captured pieces score negative, also the pieces still in your own half (now the opponents`) are considered "captured".

There were different editions of the game, a more expensive one with 3-dimensional figures and a cheaper one with flat stones instead.

In 1902 there was a second edition with simpler rules.
